Zeebe evaluates only FEEL — JUEL syntax is unrecognised, the expression evaluates to null or default behaviour (routes through default flow), depending on context. Migration must rewrite all expressions from JUEL to FEEL: `${orderAmount > 1000}` → `=orderAmount > 1000`. — JUEL→FEEL migration is canonical C7→C8 trap.

scenario:
A marketing platform sends customer engagement campaigns through multiple channels — email, SMS, push notification. Each customer can have preferences enabling one or more channels. The campaign process needs to fan out to only the enabled channels for each customer (some get all three, some get just email, some get email + SMS). After all dispatched channels complete, the campaign moves to "Track Delivery" once and only once.

The current model uses an Inclusive Gateway with three conditional flows (`=preferences.email`, `=preferences.sms`, `=preferences.push`), three Service Tasks (one per channel), and an Exclusive Gateway downstream. Customers with multiple enabled channels trigger "Track Delivery" multiple times — duplicate tracking entries appear.
question: Which BPMN join construct is correct for "wait for only the activated parallel paths to complete"?

options.text: Replace the downstream Exclusive Gateway with an Inclusive Gateway. The joining Inclusive Gateway waits specifically for the activated paths to complete (tracked by Zeebe based on upstream activation).
Replace the downstream Exclusive Gateway with a Parallel Gateway.
Convert the three Service Tasks into a Multi-Instance Subprocess.
Add a Service Task downstream that polls Operate API for parallel-task completion.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Inclusive Gateway is BPMN's conditional fork-join: at fan-out, evaluates conditions and activates only matching paths; at join, waits specifically for the paths that were activated upstream, not all paths defined. Zeebe tracks path activation state. Matches the "wait for only activated parallel paths" requirement precisely.

(b) Incorrect: Parallel Gateway always expects ALL incoming flows; would block waiting for paths that were never activated.

(c) Incorrect: Multi-Instance is for same activity repeated; three heterogeneous channel-specific Service Tasks don't fit MI.

(d) Incorrect: Operate API is eventually consistent and not for runtime control flow.

a.text: Inclusive Gateway is BPMN's conditional fork-join: at fan-out, evaluates conditions and activates only matching paths; at join, waits specifically for the paths that were activated upstream, not all paths defined. Zeebe tracks path activation state. Matches the "wait for only activated parallel paths" requirement precisely.

b.text: Parallel Gateway always expects ALL incoming flows; would block waiting for paths that were never activated. — Parallel waits for all defined;.

c.text: Multi-Instance is for same activity repeated; three heterogeneous channel-specific Service Tasks don't fit MI. — Multi-Instance same activity, heterogeneous.

d.text: Operate API is eventually consistent and not for runtime control flow. — Operate API query layer, runtime control.

scenario:
A SaaS company's subscription lifecycle process must allow the customer to upgrade their plan at any point during the subscription term (annual billing cycle). The upgrade message `planUpgraded` arrives asynchronously, correlated by `subscription_id`. Upgrade must work regardless of which stage the subscription is at (initial provisioning, active, mid-billing, renewal pending). Each subscription can be upgraded at most once per term (multiple upgrade requests in the same term should be idempotent — only the first one applies).

The main flow (provisioning → active → billing → renewal pending) should stay clean and not be cluttered with upgrade handling on every step.
question: Which BPMN construct models this asynchronous interrupt cleanly?

options.text: Wrap the main flow in a Subprocess and add an Event Subprocess with an Interrupting Message Start Event correlated on `subscription_id`. The Event Subprocess interrupts the parent scope on message arrival.
Place a Message Catch Event at the start of the process.
Attach Non-interrupting Message Boundary Events to every Service Task in the main flow.
Use an Event-Based Gateway at every transition between main-flow steps.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Event Subprocess with Interrupting Message Start Event is BPMN's canonical pattern for asynchronous "interrupt at any point in a scope". The Event Subprocess lives inside the wrapping subprocess. When `planUpgraded` arrives, Zeebe cancels whatever is active in main flow (interrupting), and the Event Subprocess's outgoing flow takes over. Single-fire semantics are default — once consumed, the message subscription closes (multiple subsequent upgrade requests for the same subscription_id in the same term don't re-trigger). Clean main flow, scope-level interrupt.

(b) Incorrect: Non-interrupting Boundary spawns a new parallel token when message arrives — main flow continues + upgrade handler runs concurrently. Upgrading a subscription while it's simultaneously being billed is the failure mode the scenario forbids.

(c) Incorrect: Message Catch at process start blocks process start until upgrade arrives — backwards semantics.

(d) Incorrect: Event-Based Gateway at every transition clutters the main flow; also it's a point-in-time choice, not continuous "any-point" interrupt.

a.text: Event Subprocess with Interrupting Message Start Event is BPMN's canonical pattern for asynchronous "interrupt at any point in a scope". The Event Subprocess lives inside the wrapping subprocess. When `planUpgraded` arrives, Zeebe cancels whatever is active in main flow (interrupting), and the Event Subprocess's outgoing flow takes over. Single-fire semantics are default — once consumed, the message subscription closes (multiple subsequent upgrade requests for the same subscription_id in the same term don't re-trigger). Clean main flow, scope-level interrupt. — Event Subprocess + Interrupting Message Start async interrupt at any point.

b.text: Message Catch at process start blocks process start until upgrade arrives — backwards semantics.

c.text: Non-interrupting Boundary spawns a new parallel token when message arrives — main flow continues + upgrade handler runs concurrently. Upgrading a subscription while it's simultaneously being billed is the failure mode the scenario forbids. — Non-interrupting spawns parallel token; upgrade + billing concurrent.

d.text: Event-Based Gateway at every transition clutters the main flow; also it's a point-in-time choice, not continuous "any-point" interrupt. — Event-Based Gateway point-in-time choice + clutter.

scenario: A health-services agency configures a User Task "Review Patient Intake" assigned to a nurse. The agency's SLA requires the task to be claimed within 2 hours; otherwise it should be flagged for management attention. The team configures the User Task with a due date property: `=now() + duration("PT2H")`. After deployment, the team observes the task appears in Tasklist correctly but doesn't visually indicate "overdue" when the 2-hour mark passes — the Tasklist view shows tasks the same way regardless of due date.
question: What is the correct understanding of due dates on User Tasks?

options.text: Due date is metadata stored on the task — Tasklist UI displays it (e.g., shows "Due in 1 hour" or "Overdue by 30 min") and can sort/filter by due date. Zeebe does NOT automatically take action on due-date expiry; for automated action (notification, escalation), attach a Timer Boundary Event to the User Task.
Due date is a deprecated property; use only Timer Boundary Events.
Due date triggers a Zeebe incident at expiry, requiring manual resolution.
Due date automatically cancels the User Task after expiry and routes to a default error path.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Due date on a User Task is metadata — Tasklist UI uses it for display ("Due in N hours", "Overdue by M") and sorting/filtering, but Zeebe does not act on expiry automatically. The team's assumption that due date triggers escalation is incorrect; due date is informational.

(b) Incorrect: Due date doesn't auto-cancel.

(c) Incorrect: Due date doesn't create incidents.

(d) Incorrect: Due date is not deprecated; it complements Timer Boundary Events.

scenario: A logistics company's BPMN process has a Service Task that calls a partner API. The partner requires a specific HTTP header `X-Partner-Account-ID` with a fixed value for the company. The team uses a generic Java Job Worker that already handles authentication; they want to pass the account ID per Service Task (different processes might use different partner accounts) without modifying the worker code.
question: Which BPMN configuration mechanism passes a per-task static value to the worker?

options.text: Hardcode the value in the worker code and select different workers per partner account.

Configure the Service Task with Task Headers — key/value pairs in the BPMN XML that are visible to the worker via `job.getCustomHeaders()` (Java) or equivalent in other SDKs. The worker reads `X-Partner-Account-ID` from headers per job.
Use Service Task input mapping with a constant FEEL value.
Set a process variable `partnerAccountId` upstream and read it from the worker via `job.getVariables()`.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Task Headers is exactly the mechanism for passing per-task static configuration to the worker without polluting process variables. Define key/value pairs in BPMN (`X-Partner-Account-ID = "ACCT-001"`); the worker receives them via `job.getCustomHeaders()`. Different Service Tasks in different processes can carry different header values without code changes — design-time configuration.

(b) Suboptimal: Process variables are for runtime data flow, not static configuration. Setting a variable upstream pollutes process state with a constant; Task Headers is cleaner.

(c) Incorrect: Hardcoding in worker requires separate worker deployments per account — anti-pattern.

(d) Working but heavier: Input mapping with a constant works but is unusual usage; Task Headers is the canonical mechanism for static per-task config.

scenario:
An order process has a Message Catch Event waiting for a `shipment_status_update` from a third-party logistics partner. The partner sends updates correlated by `tracking_number`. However, the same `tracking_number` can carry multiple updates over time (pickup, in-transit, out-for-delivery, delivered) — the process should advance only on the specific update type matching its current expectation (e.g., waiting for "delivered").

The team configures `correlationKey: =tracking_number`. All updates with the same tracking number arrive at the catch event regardless of update type, advancing the process too early.
question: Which Camunda 8 mechanism handles this "filter by update type" scenario?

options.text: Use Event Subprocess that catches all updates and routes via Exclusive Gateway.
Configure the correlation key to include both fields: `correlationKey: =tracking_number + "-" + expected_update_type` on the catch event, and have the partner publish with the same composite key.
Camunda automatically filters messages by content; the team must verify the correlation key matches.
Configure the Message Catch Event with a message name specific to update type (e.g., `shipment_delivered` instead of `shipment_status_update`) and have the partner publish with the matching name.

(b) Correct: Messages in Zeebe are matched on both name and correlation key. Using a generic message name (`shipment_status_update`) for different events is bad design — splitting into specific names (`shipment_picked_up`, `shipment_in_transit`, `shipment_delivered`) lets the catch event subscribe specifically to the type it needs. The correlation key (`=tracking_number`) still scopes to the right process instance.

(a) Working but more complex: Composite correlation key works but requires the partner to publish with the composite — coordination overhead. Message name is the cleaner abstraction.

(c) Incorrect: Camunda doesn't filter messages by arbitrary content — match is on name + correlation key.

(d) Suboptimal: Event Subprocess + downstream routing works but is heavier than message-name specificity.

scenario: A periodic data-cleanup process must run 5 times in a year — quarterly plus one ad-hoc end-of-year cleanup. The Timer Cycle Start Event should fire 5 times then stop. The team writes `cycle: R5/PT90D` expecting "repeat 5 times, every 90 days".
question: Which timer expression is correct for a bounded repetition?

options.text: `cycle: R5/P3M` — repeat 5 times, every 3 months (calendar-aware). After the 5th fire, the timer stops.
`cycle: R/P3M with limit 5` — explicit limit clause.
`cycle: 5 × P3M` — multiplication syntax.
`cycle: R5/PT90D` — repeat 5 times, every 90-day duration (fixed).

explanation:
(a) Correct: ISO 8601 cycle notation: `R{N}/{duration}` where N is the repetition count. `R5/P3M` repeats 5 times, every 3 months (period notation — calendar-aware for months). After 5 firings, the timer is exhausted and the event no longer fires.

(b) Partially correct but uses wrong notation: `PT90D` is invalid ISO 8601 — `T` prefix is for time-of-day components (PT15M = 15 minutes, PT2H = 2 hours), not days. `P90D` is correct for "90 days". Also calendar months ≠ 90 days exactly — using duration-based PT/P syntax drifts from calendar quarters.

(c) Incorrect: "with limit" is not ISO 8601 syntax.

(d) Incorrect: Multiplication syntax doesn't exist.

(b) Incorrect: No default variable name; the result is discarded.

scenario: A team's BPMN process has nested subprocesses: an outer Embedded Subprocess containing an inner Embedded Subprocess. Without explicit promotion, the inner variable is invisible outside. The outer-to-parent boundary works the same way: outer's output mapping must promote anything it wants the parent to see.

(b) Incorrect: No auto-promotion; explicit output mapping is required.

scenario: A loan-application process uses Document Handling for uploaded passport scans. The team wants documents to be auto-deleted after 90 days to comply with data retention policy. The team is wondering whether Document Handling has built-in TTL.

The team can configure 90 days at upload time to comply with retention policy.

(c) Incorrect: No 30-day hard-coded deletion; TTL is configurable.

scenario:
A customer-service platform wants to integrate an LLM for ticket triage. Two scenarios:
- Scenario A: Categorise the ticket into one of 5 predefined categories (single LLM call, structured response, no follow-up).
- Scenario B: Investigate complex tickets where the LLM may need to call search APIs, summarise findings, request human review — multi-step agentic reasoning.
question: For each scenario, which Camunda 8.8 pattern fits best?

options.text: Both scenarios use AI Agent Connector since it's the LLM-specific feature.
Scenario A: regular Service Task. Scenario B: Multi-Instance Subprocess.
Both scenarios use Ad-hoc Subprocess for consistency.
Scenario A: AI Agent Connector (single structured call). Scenario B: Ad-hoc Subprocess (dynamic multi-step agentic orchestration).

explanation:
(a) Correct: The two patterns target different use cases: - AI Agent Connector = single-element LLM invocation with structured prompt and response. Best for deterministic, single-shot LLM calls (categorisation, summarisation, draft generation). - Ad-hoc Subprocess = container with candidate inner activities; an agent (LLM-driven or rule-driven) dynamically selects which activities to run, in what order, repeatedly. Best for non-deterministic agentic orchestration.

(b) Incorrect: AI Agent Connector doesn't fit multi-step dynamic agentic reasoning — it's a single call.

(c) Incorrect: Ad-hoc Subprocess is overkill for single-shot categorisation; AI Agent Connector is cleaner.

(d) Incorrect: Regular Service Task lacks the LLM-specific structuring; Multi-Instance is wrong abstraction for dynamic agentic reasoning.

a.text: AI Agent Connector doesn't fit multi-step dynamic agentic reasoning — it's a single call. — Connector mismatches Scenario B's dynamic requirement.

b.text: Regular Service Task lacks the LLM-specific structuring; Multi-Instance is wrong abstraction for dynamic agentic reasoning. — generic Service Task misses LLM-specific feature.

c.text: Ad-hoc Subprocess is overkill for single-shot categorisation; AI Agent Connector is cleaner. — Ad-hoc overkill Scenario A.

d.text: The two patterns target different use cases: - AI Agent Connector = single-element LLM invocation with structured prompt and response. Best for deterministic, single-shot LLM calls (categorisation, summarisation, draft generation). - Ad-hoc Subprocess = container with candidate inner activities; an agent (LLM-driven or rule-driven) dynamically selects which activities to run, in what order, repeatedly. Best for non-deterministic agentic orchestration. — Right tool for each use case: structured (Connector) vs dynamic (Ad-hoc).

scenario: A Service Task's worker (Java) hits an unexpected runtime exception (NullPointerException). The worker code calls `throwError(jobKey, errorCode: null, errorMessage: "NPE encountered")` — passing `null` for the errorCode because the developer isn't sure what code to use. The Service Task has one Error Boundary Event configured with `errorCode = "PROCESSING_ERROR"`.
question: What happens at runtime?

options.text: The thrown error has no errorCode, so the boundary event (which expects "PROCESSING_ERROR") does NOT match. The error propagates upward — to enclosing subprocesses, eventually to the top level — looking for an error handler. If no handler is found, Zeebe creates an Incident.
Zeebe treats null errorCode as wildcard — matches any Error Boundary Event. The boundary fires.
Throwing error with null errorCode produces an Incident immediately, bypassing boundary events.
Zeebe substitutes a default errorCode ("UNCAUGHT") which matches only Error Boundary Events configured with that exact code.

explanation:
(a) Correct: BPMN error handling propagates upward through enclosing scopes until a matching Error Catch (boundary or event subprocess) is found. An error with a specific errorCode matches only events with that exact code; an error with no code matches a "catch-all" Error Catch (one without specified code). If neither matches at any enclosing scope, Zeebe creates an Incident. The team's boundary expecting "PROCESSING_ERROR" doesn't match an empty errorCode, so the error propagates up — and if no enclosing scope has an Error Catch, Incident is created.

(b) Incorrect: Null errorCode doesn't wildcard-match.

(c) Partially correct outcome (eventually Incident if no handler), but the mechanism description is wrong: Propagation happens first.

(d) Incorrect: No default substitution; null stays null.

a.text: BPMN error handling propagates upward through enclosing scopes until a matching Error Catch (boundary or event subprocess) is found. An error with a specific errorCode matches only events with that exact code; an error with no code matches a "catch-all" Error Catch (one without specified code). If neither matches at any enclosing scope, Zeebe creates an Incident. The team's boundary expecting "PROCESSING_ERROR" doesn't match an empty errorCode, so the error propagates up — and if no enclosing scope has an Error Catch, Incident is created. — Error propagation upward + Incident if uncaught.

b.text: Null errorCode doesn't wildcard-match.

c.verdict: Partially correct outcome (eventually Incident if no handler), but the mechanism description is wrong
c.text: Propagation happens first. — partially correct outcome but wrong mechanism description.

d.text: No default substitution; null stays null. — invented default substitution.

scenario: A travel-booking process has an Embedded Subprocess "Book Flight" that internally throws an Escalation Event with code "FLIGHT_PRICE_INCREASED" when the airline's price has changed since quote. The team wants the parent process to continue normal flow but also notify the customer of the price change via a parallel side-flow.
question: Which BPMN construct routes the Escalation to the parent for notification while keeping main flow active?

options.text: Attach a Non-interrupting Escalation Boundary Event to the Embedded Subprocess in the parent, matching code "FLIGHT_PRICE_INCREASED". When the inner throws, the boundary spawns a new parallel token following its outgoing flow (notify customer); the subprocess continues normally.
Throw the escalation as an Error event with isInterrupting=false.
Attach an Interrupting Error Boundary Event instead — Error events are the standard mechanism for inter-scope notification.
Use a Signal Throw inside the subprocess and Signal Catch in the parent.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Escalation Events are BPMN's mechanism for non-blocking notifications between scopes — different from Error events which are interrupting. A Non-interrupting Escalation Boundary Event on the parent matches the inner-thrown escalation and spawns a parallel token (notify customer flow) while leaving the inner subprocess running. The main flow continues, the parallel flow handles notification.

(b) Incorrect: Interrupting Error Boundary cancels the inner subprocess — wrong, the scenario wants to continue.

(c) Suboptimal: Signal events are global broadcasts; Escalation is the typed scope-bubble construct. Wrong abstraction.

(d) Incorrect: Error events don't have "non-interrupting" mode in BPMN spec; Escalation is the non-interrupting equivalent.

The main flow continues, the parallel flow handles notification. — Non-interrupting Escalation Boundary BPMN canonical pattern for parallel notification.

b.text: Error events don't have "non-interrupting" mode in BPMN spec; Escalation is the non-interrupting equivalent. — Error events don't have non-interrupting mode.

c.text: Interrupting Error Boundary cancels the inner subprocess — wrong, the scenario wants to continue. — Error Boundary interrupting (cancels subprocess); wrong dimension.

d.text: Signal events are global broadcasts; Escalation is the typed scope-bubble construct. Wrong abstraction. — Signal global broadcast; scope-typed.

scenario: An e-commerce platform's discount engine uses DMN to determine which discount tier applies. The rules are: (1) loyalty customers get base discount; (2) bulk orders get base discount; (3) seasonal sale adds extra. A customer could match multiple rules. The marketing team's requirement is "if multiple rules match, the order matters — earlier rules (by row position) take precedence, and the engine should return only the first match".
question: Which Hit Policy fits this "first-match-wins by row order"?

options.text: UNIQUE — exactly one rule must match; multiple matches error out.
PRIORITY — multiple matches OK; returns the highest-priority output.
FIRST — evaluates rules in row-definition order, returns the first matching rule's output, ignores later matches.
ANY — multiple matches must agree.

explanation:
(a) Correct: FIRST is the canonical Hit Policy for "first-match-wins by row order". The engine evaluates rules top-down in row position; the first rule whose conditions match has its output returned, all subsequent matches are ignored. Use when row order encodes priority semantics.

(b) Incorrect: UNIQUE forbids multiple matches — different semantics.

(c) Incorrect: PRIORITY orders by declared output priority, not row position. Different ordering dimension.

(d) Incorrect: ANY requires agreement, not first-wins.

a.text: UNIQUE forbids multiple matches — different semantics. — UNIQUE forbids multi-match.

b.text: PRIORITY orders by declared output priority, not row position. Different ordering dimension. — PRIORITY output-priority based, row.

c.text: FIRST is the canonical Hit Policy for "first-match-wins by row order". The engine evaluates rules top-down in row position; the first rule whose conditions match has its output returned, all subsequent matches are ignored. Use when row order encodes priority semantics. — FIRST BPMN-canonical for row-order first-match.

d.text: ANY requires agreement, not first-wins. — ANY enforces agreement.

scenario: A retail analytics report queries the highest discount that applied to each order. The DMN `discount-rules` matches multiple rules per order and outputs discount percentages. The team wants the final result to be the maximum discount across all matching rules (e.g., if both "loyalty" and "bulk" matched at 10% and 15%, return 15%).
question: Which Hit Policy + aggregator returns the MAX of all matching outputs?

options.text: COLLECT without aggregator + downstream FEEL `max()` in Script Task.
PRIORITY — returns highest-priority output.
COLLECT with MAX aggregator — evaluates all matches, returns the maximum output value.
OUTPUT ORDER — returns outputs sorted, take first.

explanation:
(a) Correct: COLLECT with MAX aggregator is the canonical DMN policy for "max of matching outputs". The DMN engine evaluates all matching rules, computes max of their outputs, returns single scalar. No downstream processing needed.

(b) Incorrect: PRIORITY is about output's declared priority, not numerical max value.

(c) Works but reinvents built-in aggregator: Adding a Script Task with `max()` works but COLLECT-MAX does it natively.

(d) Incorrect: OUTPUT ORDER returns sorted list, not single max.

a.text: Adding a Script Task with `max()` works but COLLECT-MAX does it natively. — works reinvents built-in.

b.text: PRIORITY is about output's declared priority, not numerical max value. — PRIORITY output priority, numeric max.

c.text: COLLECT with MAX aggregator is the canonical DMN policy for "max of matching outputs". The DMN engine evaluates all matching rules, computes max of their outputs, returns single scalar. No downstream processing needed. — COLLECT-MAX canonical aggregator.

d.text: OUTPUT ORDER returns sorted list, not single max. — sorted list, single max.

scenario: A logistics DMN determines if a package qualifies for "fragile handling". The team writes a Decision Table with two input columns: `weightKg` and `containsGlass` (boolean). The team writes one row: `weightKg = -` (any value) and `containsGlass = true` → output "fragile". For all other cases, the table should return "standard".
question: How does the team configure the "default" or "fall-through" case?

options.text: Configure the table's "default output" property to "standard".
Use an Exclusive Gateway downstream of the Business Rule Task to handle the "no rule matched" case.

Add a catch-all bottom rule with `weightKg = -` (don't-care) and `containsGlass = -` (don't-care) outputting "standard". With FIRST or PRIORITY hit policy, this fires only if no specific rule matched.
DMN automatically returns the output column's default value when no rule matches.

explanation:
(a) Correct: DMN handles defaults via a catch-all bottom rule with all input columns set to `-` (don't-care, matches anything). Combined with FIRST hit policy (or single-matching policy), the catch-all fires only when no preceding rule matched. This is the canonical idiomatic DMN approach for defaults.

(b) Incorrect: No "default output" property at table level in standard DMN.

(c) Suboptimal: Pushing default handling into BPMN works but defeats the encapsulation that DMN provides.

(d) Incorrect: DMN doesn't auto-return defaults — without a matching rule, the result is null (or empty).

scenario: A DMN decides loan-rate adjustments based on the applicant's `birthDate`. The team wants to determine if the applicant was born between 1960-01-01 and 1980-12-31 (inclusive) for a specific rate offer. The team writes the input cell for the `birthDate` column as a range.
question: Which DMN range syntax expresses "between 1960-01-01 and 1980-12-31 inclusive"?

options.text: `(date("1960-01-01")..date("1980-12-31"))` — parentheses are inclusive in DMN.
`[date("1960-01-01")..date("1980-12-31")]` — square brackets inclusive on both ends, date() built-in to parse string literals.
`>= "1960-01-01" and <= "1980-12-31"` — comparison operators with string dates.
`between date("1960-01-01") and date("1980-12-31")` — between keyword.

explanation:
(a) Correct: DMN/FEEL range syntax `[a..b]` with square brackets is inclusive on both bounds. For date ranges, use `date("YYYY-MM-DD")` to convert string literals to date type — comparisons then work on dates. `[date("1960-01-01")..date("1980-12-31")]` matches any date from Jan 1, 1960 through Dec 31, 1980, inclusive.

(b) Partially correct mechanism but wrong syntax for input cells: Comparison operators with strings work in standalone FEEL but Decision Table input cells expect abbreviated FEEL forms; comparing strings without `date()` conversion also yields lexicographic comparison, not date comparison.

(c) Incorrect: `between` is not standard DMN/FEEL syntax.

(d) Incorrect: Parentheses are exclusive in DMN range notation, not inclusive.

scenario: A DRD has Decision A that takes inputs `customerType` and `region`. Decision B depends on A and additionally takes input `orderAmount`. When invoking the DRD from a Business Rule Task with `decisionId: B`, what inputs must be supplied as process variables?
question: Which set of process variables is required?

options.text: `customerType`, `region`, `orderAmount` — all leaf-level inputs needed by the entire chain. Zeebe walks the DRD backward from B, identifies upstream A, gathers all leaf inputs (A's: customerType + region; B's additional: orderAmount).

(a) Correct: When invoking decision B which transitively depends on A, Zeebe traverses the DRD's Information Requirements backward from B to find all leaf-level Input Data. All leaf inputs needed by A AND B must be supplied as process variables. A's output is computed at evaluation time using its inputs — the caller doesn't supply A's outputs.

(b) Incorrect: A needs its own inputs (`customerType`, `region`) to compute; can't skip the upstream needs.

(c) Partially correct mechanism: Zeebe does see all process variables (scope inheritance), but only the leaf inputs are USED by the DRD. The team should ensure at least the leaf inputs are present; surplus variables are ignored.

(d) Incorrect: A's output is computed at evaluation time; doesn't need pre-population.

scenario: A global SaaS rollout deploys the same Camunda 8 onboarding form across 3 regions — EN-US, DE-DE, FR-FR. The form labels, placeholders, validation messages must be localised per region's language. The form is the same shape; only text changes.
question: Which Forms localisation approach is canonical?

options.text: Use FEEL templating in labels with a process variable `language` indicating the user's locale. Each field's label is a FEEL expression returning the appropriate string for the locale.

Camunda Forms has built-in i18n via `lang` attribute on each label.

This is the canonical i18n pattern in Camunda Forms — single form, multi-language at runtime.

(b) Suboptimal: 3 separate forms means triple maintenance — when adding a field, you update three files. Not DRY.

(c) Incorrect: No built-in `lang` attribute mechanism.

(d) Incorrect: Forms support i18n via FEEL templating.

This is the canonical i18n pattern in Camunda Forms — single form, multi-language at runtime. — FEEL templating + language variable canonical i18n.

scenario: A team's incident-management process notifies on-call engineers via Slack when a critical error fires. They use the Slack Outbound Connector from Camunda's catalog. The Slack workspace's webhook URL is sensitive and must not appear in BPMN XML.
question: How should the Slack URL be configured?

options.text: Slack Connector has a special `webhookUrl` property that's encrypted at rest in BPMN.
Hardcode it in the Slack Connector property; cluster encryption automatically protects BPMN XML.

Store the webhook URL as a cluster secret (via Console UI for SaaS, or via env/K8s/Vault for SM) and reference it in the Slack Connector property as `{{secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL}}`. The runtime resolves the placeholder at execution.
Pass the URL via a process variable set by a Service Task that reads from environment.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Cluster Secrets + placeholder syntax is the canonical secret-handling pattern across all Connectors. Define the webhook URL as a secret named `SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL` in Console UI (SaaS) or via configured provider (SM). Reference in the Connector config as `{{secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL}}`. BPMN XML contains only the placeholder — safe to commit to Git.

(b) Incorrect: BPMN XML is not auto-encrypted; hardcoded values leak via Git/audit.

(c) Suboptimal: Process-variable-passing leaks the URL to audit logs / Operate / downstream tasks.

(d) Incorrect: No special encrypted-property mechanism in BPMN.

a.text: No special encrypted-property mechanism in BPMN. — invented mechanism.

b.text: BPMN XML is not auto-encrypted; hardcoded values leak via Git/audit. — no auto-encryption.

c.text: Cluster Secrets + placeholder syntax is the canonical secret-handling pattern across all Connectors. BPMN XML contains only the placeholder — safe to commit to Git. — Cluster Secrets + placeholder canonical Connector secret pattern.

scenario: A team integrates with AWS — when an SQS message arrives on a configured queue, a Camunda 8 process should start. They configure the AWS SQS Inbound Connector with the queue ARN and AWS credentials (via secrets). After deploying, the team observes that messages on the SQS queue are consumed by the Connector but process instances are not started.
question: What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The Connector requires a process start configuration — specifying which process definition to start, and how to extract correlation keys / variables from the SQS message. Without this, the Connector consumes messages but doesn't trigger anything.
SQS queue must be FIFO; standard queues aren't supported.
Inbound Connectors only support Message Catch Events, not Process Start.
AWS credentials are wrong; SQS messages are silently discarded.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Inbound Connectors (whether webhook, SQS, Kafka, etc.) require proper wiring to a BPMN element — typically either a Process Start Event (creates new instance) or a Message Catch Event (correlates to existing instance). The Connector configuration must include: which BPMN process to start (if Start Event) and how to extract message data into process variables. Without this configuration, the Connector successfully consumes messages but has no target to trigger.

(b) Incorrect: Bad credentials would produce errors visible in logs, not silent discard.

(c) Incorrect: Inbound Connectors support both Start Events and Catch Events.

(d) Incorrect: SQS Connector supports both standard and FIFO queues.

a.text: Inbound Connectors (whether webhook, SQS, Kafka, etc.) require proper wiring to a BPMN element — typically either a Process Start Event (creates new instance) or a Message Catch Event (correlates to existing instance). The Connector configuration must include: which BPMN process to start (if Start Event) and how to extract message data into process variables. Without this configuration, the Connector successfully consumes messages but has no target to trigger. — Connector wiring to BPMN element mandatory configuration.

b.text: SQS Connector supports both standard and FIFO queues. — both queue types supported.

c.text: Inbound Connectors support both Start Events and Catch Events. — both supported.

d.text: Bad credentials would produce errors visible in logs, not silent discard. — bad creds produce visible errors, silent discard.

scenario: A team built a Custom Outbound Connector in 2024 against Camunda 8.5 SDK. They're now upgrading to Camunda 8.8 SaaS. They want to know if the existing Connector JAR can be uploaded to the 8.8 cluster's Connector Runtime, or if it needs rebuilding.
question: What is the correct understanding of Connector SDK version compatibility?

options.text: Always requires rebuild — minor versions break binary compatibility.
Forever compatible; no testing needed for any version bump.

Test in staging first. The Connector SDK has stability commitments across minor versions; an 8.5-compatible Connector typically works on 8.8 but may benefit from recompilation against the matching SDK version. Major version jumps (e.g., to a hypothetical 9.x) may break compatibility.
Custom Connectors aren't portable; rewrite per-cluster.

explanation:
(a) Correct: SDK provides stability commitments across minor versions (8.5 → 8.8). Practical approach: test the existing JAR on 8.8 staging cluster. If it works, deploy; if it doesn't, recompile against 8.8 SDK. Major version bumps may require code changes. Always test before production rollout.

(b) Overstated: Minor versions usually maintain binary compatibility.

(c) Understated: Forever-compatible is unrealistic; testing is always prudent.

(d) Incorrect: Connectors are portable across cluster types.

a.text: Minor versions usually maintain binary compatibility. — overstated; minor versions usually compatible.

b.text: Forever-compatible is unrealistic; testing is always prudent. — overly optimistic.

c.text: SDK provides stability commitments across minor versions (8.5 → 8.8). Practical approach: test the existing JAR on 8.8 staging cluster. If it works, deploy; if it doesn't, recompile against 8.8 SDK. Major version bumps may require code changes. Always test before production rollout. — Test in staging + minor-version stability practical answer.

d.text: Connectors are portable across cluster types. — Connectors portable.

scenario: An Outbound HTTP Connector calls a partner API. The request body must include data from process variables (customer ID, order details) plus some computed fields (current timestamp, signature hash). The team wants to compose the request body cleanly without manual JSON string construction.
question: Which Connector configuration handles structured request body composition?

options.text: Use FEEL expression in the Connector's Body field to construct a context (object) literal. Example: `={customerId: customer.id, order: order, timestamp: now(), signature: hash(customer.id + order.total)}`. The FEEL evaluator produces a JSON-serialisable object.
Connector body must be a static JSON string; computed fields aren't supported.
Build the JSON manually in a Script Task before the Connector.
Use process variables directly; Connector interpolates them with `${var}` syntax.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Outbound HTTP Connector accepts a FEEL context expression in its Body field for structured composition. The FEEL evaluator builds the object at runtime — process variables, computed fields, FEEL built-ins (like `now()`), nested objects all work. The runtime serializes the object to JSON. Cleaner than string concatenation.

(b) Working but ugly: Script Task to build JSON works but pollutes the BPMN with an extra task; FEEL handles this inline.

(c) Incorrect: Computed fields supported via FEEL.

(d) Incorrect: Camunda 8 uses FEEL (`=`), not JUEL (`${}`).

a.text: Outbound HTTP Connector accepts a FEEL context expression in its Body field for structured composition. The FEEL evaluator builds the object at runtime — process variables, computed fields, FEEL built-ins (like `now()`), nested objects all work. The runtime serializes the object to JSON. Cleaner than string concatenation. — FEEL context expression canonical for structured body composition.

b.text: Computed fields supported via FEEL. — computed fields supported.

c.text: Script Task to build JSON works but pollutes the BPMN with an extra task; FEEL handles this inline. — works adds extra task.

d.text: Camunda 8 uses FEEL (`=`), not JUEL (`${}`). — JUEL syntax C8.

scenario: A FEEL expression in a Service Task's input mapping computes a discount: `discount = if customer.tier = "GOLD" then 0.15 else if customer.tier = "SILVER" then 0.10 else 0.05`. At runtime, when `customer.tier` is null, the FEEL evaluator's behavior is observed.
question: Which best describes FEEL semantics for null in this if-elif-else?

options.text: `null = "GOLD"` returns `null` (not `false`). The `if null then ... else ...` falls through to the else branch returning `0.05`. The expression evaluates without raising an exception, but a `null = "GOLD"` is conceptually "unknown not equal."
Returns `null` from the entire expression; discount becomes null.
Raises an evaluation error; the Service Task creates an Incident.
Returns `0.15` because null is treated as a default match.

explanation:
(a) Correct: FEEL's `if` statement evaluates the condition; if it's not strictly `true`, the else branch executes. `null = "GOLD"` returns `null`, which in `if` context is treated as falsy → fall through to else. No exception, expression returns `0.05`.

(b) Incorrect: FEEL `=` on null is graceful, not exception-raising for this case.

(c) Incorrect: else branch executes; result is 0.05 not null.

(d) Incorrect: null doesn't match GOLD or SILVER.

a.text: FEEL's `if` statement evaluates the condition; if it's not strictly `true`, the else branch executes. `null = "GOLD"` returns `null`, which in `if` context is treated as falsy → fall through to else. No exception, expression returns `0.05`. — FEEL falsy-on-null + else fall-through.

b.text: else branch executes; result is 0.05 not null. — else branch runs.

c.text: FEEL `=` on null is graceful, not exception-raising for this case. — no exception for this case.

d.text: null doesn't match GOLD or SILVER.

scenario: A FEEL expression filters a list of orders to keep only those over $1000: `large_orders = [o in orders : o.total > 1000]`. The team wants to extend this — they also want to know the total count of large orders.
question: Which FEEL approach computes both the filtered list and its count?

options.text: FEEL doesn't support counting filtered lists; do it in a Service Task.
`count = orders[total > 1000].count` (property access).
`large_orders = orders[total > 1000]` (filter shorthand) and `count = count(large_orders)`. FEEL provides `count(list)` built-in for list cardinality.
`count = length(large_orders)`.

explanation:
(a) Correct: FEEL has two equivalent filter syntaxes: `[item in list : condition]` and shorthand `list[condition]`. For counting, the built-in is `count(list)` — returns number of elements. Two-step expression captures both.

(b) Incorrect: FEEL supports this natively.

(c) Incorrect: FEEL lists don't expose a `.count` property; use `count(list)`.

(d) Incorrect: FEEL uses `count(list)`, not `length(list)`. (`string length(s)` exists for strings.)

a.text: FEEL supports this natively. — FEEL supports natively.

b.text: FEEL lists don't expose a `.count` property; use `count(list)`. — invented property.

c.text: FEEL has two equivalent filter syntaxes: `[item in list : condition]` and shorthand `list[condition]`. For counting, the built-in is `count(list)` — returns number of elements. Two-step expression captures both. — count() built-in list cardinality.

d.text: FEEL uses `count(list)`, not `length(list)`. (`string length(s)` exists for strings.) — close-but-wrong function name.

scenario: A FEEL date computation determines whether a contract's `start_date` is at least 30 days before `end_date`. The dates come as ISO strings from a process variable.
question: Which FEEL expression correctly performs the date arithmetic?

options.text: `date(end_date) - date(start_date) >= duration("P30D")`. FEEL's `date(string)` parses ISO date; subtraction returns days-and-time duration; `duration("P30D")` is a 30-day duration. Comparison works between durations.
FEEL doesn't support date arithmetic; do this in a Service Task.
`(end_date - start_date) > 30`.
`daysBetween(start_date, end_date) >= 30`.

explanation:
(a) Correct: FEEL's `date(string)` parses ISO date strings to date values. Subtraction of two dates yields a `days and time duration`. Comparison with `duration("P30D")` (ISO 8601 duration) tests whether elapsed time is ≥ 30 days.

(b) Incorrect: Subtracting raw strings doesn't work — they're not numbers.

(c) Incorrect: No `daysBetween` built-in in FEEL (Java/legacy Camunda 7 expression libraries had something similar).

(d) Incorrect: FEEL has rich temporal arithmetic.

a.text: FEEL's `date(string)` parses ISO date strings to date values. Subtraction of two dates yields a `days and time duration`. Comparison with `duration("P30D")` (ISO 8601 duration) tests whether elapsed time is ≥ 30 days. — date() + duration() + subtraction canonical FEEL temporal.

b.text: FEEL has rich temporal arithmetic. — FEEL has temporal support.

c.text: Subtracting raw strings doesn't work — they're not numbers. — string arithmetic.

d.text: No `daysBetween` built-in in FEEL (Java/legacy Camunda 7 expression libraries had something similar). — invented function.

scenario: A Java Job Worker built with Spring Zeebe handles `payment-processing` Service Tasks. The worker is annotated with `@JobWorker(type = "payment-processing")`. Under heavy load, the team observes the worker times out activated jobs (jobs return to the broker, re-activate, repeat).
question: Which Spring Zeebe `@JobWorker` parameter primarily controls how long the worker has before the job times out?

options.text: `maxRetries` — controls how many times job retries on failure.
`pollInterval` — how often the worker polls for jobs.

`timeout` — sets the job-activation timeout (typical default 5 min). If the worker doesn't complete/fail/handle the job within this window, Zeebe re-activates it. Increase to match real processing time.
`requestTimeout` — gRPC connection timeout.

explanation:
(a) Correct: `@JobWorker(type="...", timeout=...)` sets the job-activation timeout — how long Zeebe waits for the worker to handle this job before re-activating it. If your payment processing takes 8 minutes but default is 5, jobs time out and re-activate, causing the symptom observed.

(b) Different concern: maxRetries handles failure retry, not timeout.

(c) Different concern: pollInterval controls polling frequency.

(d) Different concern: requestTimeout handles gRPC-level timeouts, not job processing.

a.text: maxRetries handles failure retry, not timeout. — different concept (retry on failure).

b.text: pollInterval controls polling frequency. — different concept (polling).

c.text: `@JobWorker(type="...", timeout=...)` sets the job-activation timeout — how long Zeebe waits for the worker to handle this job before re-activating it. If your payment processing takes 8 minutes but default is 5, jobs time out and re-activate, causing the symptom observed. — timeout job-activation duration.

d.text: requestTimeout handles gRPC-level timeouts, not job processing. — different concept (gRPC).

scenario: A TypeScript Job Worker (using `@camunda8/sdk`) handles `image-thumbnail` Service Tasks. The Service Task expects two outputs: `thumbnailUrl` and `width`. The handler computes these and wants to return them to Zeebe.

options.text: Both a and b work — return object is auto-completed; explicit complete() is also supported.
Return an object `{ thumbnailUrl, width }` — the SDK serializes it as the job's complete-payload and Zeebe merges into process variables per Output Mapping.
Call `job.complete({ variables: { thumbnailUrl, width } })` explicitly; do not return a value.
Set Process variables via API call; the handler return is ignored.

explanation:
(d) Correct: `@camunda8/sdk` supports both ergonomic styles. Return an object → SDK auto-completes the job with those as variables. Or call `job.complete({variables: {...}})` explicitly for fine control (e.g., custom complete options). Both are documented and supported.

(a) Partially correct: Return-object style works, but explicit complete() also works.

(b) Partially correct: Explicit complete() works, but return-object also works.

(c) Incorrect: Worker handler is the completion mechanism.

a.text: `@camunda8/sdk` supports both ergonomic styles. Return an object → SDK auto-completes the job with those as variables. Or call `job.complete({variables: {...}})` explicitly for fine control (e.g., custom complete options). Both are documented and supported. — both styles supported.

b.text: Return-object style works, but explicit complete() also works. — partial — return works, but explicit also valid.

c.text: Explicit complete() works, but return-object also works. — partial — explicit works, but return-object also valid.

d.text: Worker handler is the completion mechanism. — worker IS the completion.

scenario: A team wants to start process instances programmatically from a backend service. They have credentials for the cluster's REST API. The process model is `customer-onboarding` and they need to pass `customerId` and `email` as initial variables.
question: Which API and endpoint pattern starts the instance?

options.text: Web Modeler API to deploy the process and then start.
Optimize API has a process-start endpoint.
Administration API for cluster-level start operations.
Orchestration Cluster REST API, POST `/v2/process-instances` with body `{"processDefinitionId": "customer-onboarding", "variables": {...}}`.

explanation:

POST `/v2/process-instances` creates a new instance for the specified processDefinitionId (or processDefinitionKey), with initial variables. This API replaces the legacy Tasklist/Operate APIs for instance start.

(b) Wrong API: Web Modeler API handles modeling and deployment, not instance lifecycle.

(c) Wrong API: Administration API handles cluster-level admin (members, clusters, regions), not instance start.

(d) Wrong API: Optimize is read-only analytics.

b.text: Optimize is read-only analytics. — wrong API surface.

scenario: A custom Outbound Connector authored with the Connector SDK must call a partner API that returns large response payloads (5-50 MB). The team is concerned about memory usage and timeouts.
question: Which Connector SDK feature/strategy handles large response payloads efficiently?

options.text: Return only summary headers; ignore body.
Stream the response and use Document Handling — store the large payload as a Camunda Document and pass the document reference (not the body) as a Connector output.
Connector SDK doesn't support large payloads; use a Job Worker instead.
Return the full payload as a process variable string.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Camunda 8.6+ Document Handling provides first-class document-as-variable support. For large Connector responses, the Connector code creates a Document (stores in configured storage) and returns the document reference as the variable. Process variables stay small; downstream tasks can read the document when needed.

(b) Bad practice: Multi-MB process variables degrade broker performance and bloat state.

(c) Incorrect: SDK supports this scenario via Document Handling.

(d) Suboptimal: Loses data; not a valid solution.

b.text: Camunda 8.6+ Document Handling provides first-class document-as-variable support. Process variables stay small; downstream tasks can read the document when needed. — Document Handling canonical large payloads.

c.text: SDK supports this scenario via Document Handling. — SDK supports this.

d.text: Multi-MB process variables degrade broker performance and bloat state. — leads to bloated state.

scenario: A Java Job Worker handles `validate-document` Service Tasks. The validation produces multiple possible outcomes: SUCCESS, INVALID_FORMAT, EXPIRED, MISSING_DATA. Each outcome should route to a different BPMN path. The team wants the BPMN to use exclusive gateway routing on a clean `validationStatus` variable.
question: Which Job Worker handler pattern produces the cleanest BPMN?

options.text: Worker throws a BPMN Error with errorCode for non-success cases; Error Boundary Event catches each errorCode.
Worker returns full validation report (~50 fields); BPMN gateway evaluates complex FEEL on it.
Worker fails the job with retries=0 for non-success; ops manually resolves.
Worker returns `{validationStatus: "SUCCESS"}` (or "INVALID_FORMAT" / "EXPIRED" / etc.) as job-complete variables. The BPMN Exclusive Gateway then checks `=validationStatus = "SUCCESS"` etc.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Clean separation of concerns: Worker computes the business outcome and returns a simple status enum + supporting variables. BPMN gateway routes on the status. The diagram is readable, business analysts see the routing logic clearly.

(b) Different semantics: BPMN Errors are for exceptional cases, not normal business outcomes. Treating "INVALID_FORMAT" as an error makes the BPMN harder to read and conflates error handling with normal routing.

(c) Bad practice: Complex FEEL on raw validation reports in the gateway puts business logic in the wrong place.

(d) Wrong semantics: Failing the job creates Incidents; non-success is normal business outcome, not failure.

a.text: BPMN Errors are for exceptional cases, not normal business outcomes. Treating "INVALID_FORMAT" as an error makes the BPMN harder to read and conflates error handling with normal routing. — works but conflates errors with normal outcomes.

b.text: Complex FEEL on raw validation reports in the gateway puts business logic in the wrong place. — gateway logic too complex.

c.text: Failing the job creates Incidents; non-success is normal business outcome, not failure. — wrong semantics for business outcome.

d.text: Clean separation of concerns: Worker computes the business outcome and returns a simple status enum + supporting variables. BPMN gateway routes on the status. The diagram is readable, business analysts see the routing logic clearly. — Status enum + Exclusive Gateway clean pattern.

scenario: A team uses Spring Zeebe to write a Job Worker that handles `archive-document` Service Tasks. The handler does its work but doesn't need to return any variables — the success itself is the signal. They want the simplest possible handler.

scenario: A team builds a custom Inbound Connector that listens for events from RabbitMQ. The Connector should be deployable as a JAR to the Connector Runtime. The team is unfamiliar with the SDK structure.
question: Which is the main SDK interface that an Inbound Connector must implement?

options.text: `OutboundConnectorFunction` — handles both inbound and outbound.

`InboundConnectorExecutable` (or its newer variants) — defines `activate(InboundConnectorContext)` and `deactivate()` lifecycle methods that the Connector Runtime calls when subscribing/unsubscribing to events.
No interface needed; Connectors are just POJOs.
`ZeebeJobWorker` — subclass for inbound.

Implement `activate()` to subscribe to the external event source (RabbitMQ queue), and `deactivate()` to release resources when the process is undeployed. The Connector Runtime calls these.

(b) Wrong interface: `OutboundConnectorFunction` is for outbound; inbound has its own interface.

(c) Wrong interface: Job Workers are a different abstraction; Inbound Connectors run in Connector Runtime.

(d) Incorrect: SDK requires interface implementation.

b.text: The Connector SDK defines `InboundConnectorExecutable` (and subtypes for specific event-handling shapes like `MessageInboundConnectorExecutable`). Implement `activate()` to subscribe to the external event source (RabbitMQ queue), and `deactivate()` to release resources when the process is undeployed. The Connector Runtime calls these. — InboundConnectorExecutable the interface.

d.text: Job Workers are a different abstraction; Inbound Connectors run in Connector Runtime. — wrong abstraction (Job Worker).

scenario: A team needs to invoke a legacy desktop application (no API, only GUI) as part of a Camunda 8 process — open Excel, fill a template, save and email it. Standard Connectors can't do this; the GUI must be driven.
question: Which Camunda 8 feature is purpose-built for this scenario?

options.text: RPA (Robotic Process Automation) — Camunda 8 has RPA workers that drive UI automation (mouse clicks, keyboard input, GUI manipulation). RPA tasks are configured as Service Tasks with type `camunda::rpa`, and a deployed bot script handles the GUI work.

Manual User Task with instructions for the human to do it.
