(b) Workable but more work: Custom Job Worker with Selenium can do this but reinvents what RPA provides natively.

(c) Incorrect: AI Agent Connector is for LLM-driven decisions, not GUI manipulation.

(d) Defeats automation goal: Manual User Task is the opposite of automation.

a.text: Camunda 8 RPA is the purpose-built feature for UI automation of legacy systems. A bot script (written in RPA DSL) is deployed to an RPA worker that subscribes to RPA-typed Service Tasks. The bot performs GUI actions; process flow continues when bot completes. — RPA purpose-built UI automation.

b.text: AI Agent Connector is for LLM-driven decisions, not GUI manipulation. — wrong feature (LLM ≠ GUI).

c.text: Custom Job Worker with Selenium can do this but reinvents what RPA provides natively. — workable but reinvents wheel.

d.text: Manual User Task is the opposite of automation. — defeats automation purpose.

scenario: A FEEL expression on a Service Task input mapping checks whether a customer is VIP based on multiple criteria: account age > 5 years OR total spend > $50,000. The expression must combine these with OR.
question: Which FEEL syntax is correct for combining boolean conditions with OR?

options.text: `accountAgeYears > 5 or totalSpend > 50000` — FEEL uses lowercase keywords `and`, `or`, `not()`.
`accountAgeYears > 5 OR totalSpend > 50000` — uppercase keyword.
`accountAgeYears > 5 || totalSpend > 50000` — pipe-pipe like JavaScript.
`or(accountAgeYears > 5, totalSpend > 50000)` — function-call form.

explanation:
(a) Correct: FEEL uses lowercase keywords `and`, `or`, `not`. (`not` is also a function: `not(boolean)`.) The expression `accountAgeYears > 5 or totalSpend > 50000` is valid FEEL — produces a boolean.

(b) Incorrect: `||` is JavaScript/C-family, not FEEL.

(c) Incorrect: FEEL is case-sensitive; uppercase OR is invalid.

(d) Misleading: While FEEL has built-in functions like `any(list)` for "true if any element is true," there's no `or(a, b)` function form for two booleans. Use the operator.

a.text: FEEL uses lowercase keywords `and`, `or`, `not`. (`not` is also a function: `not(boolean)`.) The expression `accountAgeYears > 5 or totalSpend > 50000` is valid FEEL — produces a boolean. — lowercase `or` canonical.

b.text: FEEL is case-sensitive; uppercase OR is invalid.

c.text: `||` is JavaScript/C-family, not FEEL. — JS syntax FEEL.

d.text: While FEEL has built-in functions like `any(list)` for "true if any element is true," there's no `or(a, b)` function form for two booleans. Use the operator. — invented function form.

scenario: A team's RPA bot opens a CRM web app, scrapes customer data, and returns it to the Camunda process. The bot occasionally fails (e.g., page didn't load fast enough). The team wants the process to retry the RPA task automatically up to 3 times before incident-ing.
question: Which configuration handles RPA retry semantics?

options.text: Retries aren't supported for RPA; failures always create incidents.
RPA has its own retry mechanism configured in the bot script.
Wrap the RPA task in a Boundary Error Event that loops back.

Set `zeebe:taskDefinition retries="3"` on the RPA-typed Service Task. Zeebe re-activates the job on bot failure up to the configured retries; after exhaustion an Incident fires. Same Job Worker retry mechanism applies to RPA. Documentation: [Job Workers](https://docs.camunda.io/docs/components/concepts/job-workers/) + [RPA](https://docs.camunda.io/docs/components/rpa/overview/)

explanation:
(a) Correct: RPA tasks use the same job-worker retry semantics as any Service Task. `retries="3"` (set in Web Modeler "Retries" property) means Zeebe re-activates the job on bot failure up to 3 times. Bot fails on activation 1 → retry → activation 2 → fail → retry → activation 3 → fail → Incident. Set retries based on expected transient-failure probability.

(b) Different scope: Bot-script internal retries (loops within the script) are possible but separate from job-level retries; the question asks about Zeebe-level retry.

(c) Workable: Boundary Error + loop is BPMN-level pattern; works but the simpler answer is built-in retries.

(d) Incorrect: Retries are supported.

a.text: Retries are supported. — retries supported.

b.text: Bot-script internal retries (loops within the script) are possible but separate from job-level retries; the question asks about Zeebe-level retry. — different scope (script-level).

c.text: Boundary Error + loop is BPMN-level pattern; works but the simpler answer is built-in retries. — works but unnecessary BPMN complexity.

d.text: RPA tasks use the same job-worker retry semantics as any Service Task. `retries="3"` (set in Web Modeler "Retries" property) means Zeebe re-activates the job on bot failure up to 3 times. Bot fails on activation 1 → retry → activation 2 → fail → retry → activation 3 → fail → Incident. Set retries based on expected transient-failure probability. — retries attribute task definition.

scenario: A team wants to invoke an external system that requires asynchronous interaction — they send a request, then receive the response via a callback (webhook) hours later when the external system finishes. The Camunda process should wait for the callback before continuing.
question: Which BPMN + Connector pattern fits asynchronous callback?

options.text: Increase the Service Task's job-activation timeout to hours; the worker blocks.
Use a User Task that waits indefinitely.

Use Send Task (or Service Task with Outbound Connector) to send the request, followed by an Intermediate Message Catch Event with a correlation key (e.g., correlationKey = requestId). When the callback arrives, an Inbound Connector (webhook) correlates the message to the waiting instance. Documentation: [Message Events](https://docs.camunda.io/docs/components/modeler/bpmn/message-events/) + [Inbound Connectors](https://docs.camunda.io/docs/components/connectors/use-connectors/)
Use a single Service Task that polls the external system every minute for hours.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Asynchronous-callback pattern is canonical async correlation: Send Task triggers the external request (or Service Task with outbound HTTP), then Intermediate Message Catch Event waits with correlation key. When callback arrives at the Inbound webhook Connector, the Connector publishes a Message with matching correlation key → Zeebe correlates to the waiting instance → flow continues.

(b) Wastes resources: Polling for hours burns Job Worker capacity.

(c) Wrong semantics: User Tasks are for human work; callbacks are system events.

(d) Bad practice: Long-blocking workers are anti-pattern (resource hold, fragile).

a.text: Long-blocking workers are anti-pattern (resource hold, fragile). — anti-pattern.

b.text: User Tasks are for human work; callbacks are system events. — wrong semantics (User Task ≠ system event).

c.text: Asynchronous-callback pattern is canonical async correlation: Send Task triggers the external request (or Service Task with outbound HTTP), then Intermediate Message Catch Event waits with correlation key. When callback arrives at the Inbound webhook Connector, the Connector publishes a Message with matching correlation key → Zeebe correlates to the waiting instance → flow continues. — Send + Message Catch + correlation canonical async pattern.

d.text: Polling for hours burns Job Worker capacity. — wastes resources.

scenario: A team of 5 modelers collaborates on a `claim-processing` BPMN in Web Modeler SaaS. Two designers open the same file simultaneously. One adds a Service Task; the other adds an Exclusive Gateway. They save within seconds of each other.
question: Which Web Modeler collaboration behavior applies?

options.text: Web Modeler uses optimistic save — last-write-wins overwrites the first save.
Web Modeler requires the second editor to "fork" the file.

Web Modeler supports real-time collaborative editing — both modelers see each other's changes live (similar to Google Docs). Cursors and presence are shown; conflicts on the same element are arbitrated by Web Modeler.
Web Modeler is single-editor; the second person sees a "file locked" message.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Web Modeler supports real-time collaborative editing — multiple users edit the same file simultaneously with presence indicators, live updates, conflict arbitration. Designed for team modeling without manual lock/check-out flows.

(b) Incorrect: Single-editor locking is a different paradigm; Web Modeler is collaborative.

(c) Incorrect: Web Modeler arbitrates concurrent edits, not last-write-wins.

(d) Incorrect: No fork-required model.

a.text: Web Modeler arbitrates concurrent edits, not last-write-wins.

b.text: No fork-required model. — invented fork model.

c.text: Web Modeler supports real-time collaborative editing — multiple users edit the same file simultaneously with presence indicators, live updates, conflict arbitration. Designed for team modeling without manual lock/check-out flows. — Real-time collaboration the model.

d.text: Single-editor locking is a different paradigm; Web Modeler is collaborative. — single-editor paradigm.

scenario: A team's Java backend service needs to call the Orchestration Cluster REST API to start process instances. The cluster is a SaaS cluster with OAuth2 client-credentials authentication. The team needs to obtain a token.
question: Which approach correctly fetches an OAuth2 token for the cluster API?

options.text: No authentication needed for the Orchestration Cluster API; it's open.
Use the cluster's username/password basic auth.
Use an API key passed as a query parameter.

Use Camunda's documented OAuth2 flow: POST to the Auth0 token endpoint with `client_id`, `client_secret`, `audience` (the cluster's audience URL), and `grant_type=client_credentials`. The response contains an access token; pass it as `Authorization: Bearer <token>` to the API.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Camunda 8 SaaS uses OAuth2 client-credentials grant: provision an API Client in Console for the cluster, get client_id + client_secret, exchange for access token at the auth endpoint, use as Bearer token. Standard OAuth2 flow.

(b) Incorrect: Username/password basic auth is not the SaaS authentication model.

(c) Incorrect: All cluster APIs require auth.

(d) Incorrect: Camunda 8 SaaS uses OAuth2 Bearer tokens, not query params.

a.text: All cluster APIs require auth. — auth required.

b.text: Username/password basic auth is not the SaaS authentication model. — wrong auth model.

c.text: Camunda 8 SaaS uses OAuth2 Bearer tokens, not query params. — wrong auth model.

d.text: Camunda 8 SaaS uses OAuth2 client-credentials grant: provision an API Client in Console for the cluster, get client_id + client_secret, exchange for access token at the auth endpoint, use as Bearer token. Standard OAuth2 flow. — OAuth2 client-credentials canonical.

scenario: A process instance has an active Incident because a Service Task's worker repeatedly fails (NullPointerException in handler). The team fixes the worker code and redeploys. The process instance now needs to resume from the failed point — restart the worker activation.
question: Which Operate action resumes a process instance after fixing the underlying cause?

options.text: Cancel and restart the process instance.
Modify the instance to skip the failed task.
Redeploy the BPMN; existing instances auto-resume.
Resolve the Incident in Operate. This sets the job's retries back to the configured value (e.g., 3) and Zeebe re-activates the job; the now-fixed worker picks it up and completes.

explanation:
(a) Correct: "Resolve Incident" in Operate is the canonical action: sets job retries back to configured value, Zeebe re-activates the job, the fixed worker (now running with patched code) picks up and processes successfully. Instance continues from where it failed.

(b) Overkill: Cancelling loses all process state and history; Resolve Incident is cleaner.

(c) Wrong action: Modify to skip is for cases where you don't want to retry — not the standard "fix-and-retry" flow.

(d) Misconception: Redeploying the BPMN doesn't auto-resume incidents; you must explicitly resolve.

a.text: Cancelling loses all process state and history; Resolve Incident is cleaner. — overkill (loses state).

b.text: Modify to skip is for cases where you don't want to retry — not the standard "fix-and-retry" flow. — wrong action (skip ≠ retry).

c.text: Redeploying the BPMN doesn't auto-resume incidents; you must explicitly resolve. — misconception (deployment resolves).

d.text: "Resolve Incident" in Operate is the canonical action: sets job retries back to configured value, Zeebe re-activates the job, the fixed worker (now running with patched code) picks up and processes successfully. Instance continues from where it failed. — Resolve Incident canonical recovery action.

scenario: A production process model `order-fulfillment` v1 has 500 active instances. The team wants to deploy v2 with a fixed bug in a downstream Service Task. They want existing instances to continue with v1 logic until completion, but new instances to use v2.
question: Which Camunda 8 deployment behavior fits this scenario?

options.text: Must explicitly migrate each instance one-by-one.
Cancel all v1 instances; only one version can exist.

Deploy v2 — by default, already-running instances continue on their original version (v1) until completion. New instances created after v2 deployment use v2. No migration is required for this scenario.
All running instances auto-migrate to v2 upon deployment; old version disappears.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Zeebe's default versioning: when you deploy v2 of a process with the same `id`, running instances pinned on v1 continue executing the v1 model. New instance starts after v2 deployment use v2. This is the safe default — bug fixes don't disrupt in-flight work.

(b) Incorrect: No auto-migration; this would be unsafe for many fixes.

(c) Misleading: Migration is supported but not required for this scenario.

(d) Incorrect: Multiple versions coexist by design.

a.text: Migration is supported but not required for this scenario. — misleading (not required).

b.text: Multiple versions coexist by design. — versions coexist.

c.text: Zeebe's default versioning: when you deploy v2 of a process with the same `id`, running instances pinned on v1 continue executing the v1 model. New instance starts after v2 deployment use v2. This is the safe default — bug fixes don't disrupt in-flight work. — Default versioning preserves running instances.

d.text: No auto-migration; this would be unsafe for many fixes. — no auto-migration.

scenario: A process model has 4 Service Tasks. The team is testing it before production: they want to execute the process step-by-step without deploying to a cluster — verifying logic, FEEL expressions, gateway routing — using sample variables.
question: Which Camunda 8 feature enables this local simulation?

options.text: No simulation; must deploy to a dev cluster.
Operate's "Test Run" tab on the process definition.
Tasklist's simulation mode.
Play mode in Web Modeler — execute the BPMN against a sandbox runtime without deploying to production. Step through tasks, inject variables, observe routing. Useful for validation before deployment.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Play in Web Modeler is the canonical pre-deployment simulator. It runs the BPMN in a sandbox cluster, lets the modeler set initial variables, executes tasks step-by-step with mocked outputs (for tasks that would call real workers), and visualises flow execution.

(b) Incorrect: Operate is for live monitoring, not simulation; "Test Run" doesn't exist as a feature there.

(c) Incorrect: Tasklist is for User Tasks; no simulation mode.

(d) Outdated: Play replaces the dev-cluster-only workflow.

a.text: Play replaces the dev-cluster-only workflow. — outdated (Play replaces).

b.text: Operate is for live monitoring, not simulation; "Test Run" doesn't exist as a feature there. — Operate live monitoring.

c.text: Tasklist is for User Tasks; no simulation mode. — Tasklist runtime task UI.

d.text: Play in Web Modeler is the canonical pre-deployment simulator. It runs the BPMN in a sandbox cluster, lets the modeler set initial variables, executes tasks step-by-step with mocked outputs (for tasks that would call real workers), and visualises flow execution. — Play mode canonical pre-deploy simulator.

scenario: A team's process model has accumulated 12 versions across 6 months. Looking at v8, the team wants to compare it against v7 to see what changed.
question: Which Web Modeler feature shows version-to-version differences?

options.text: Git-style external diff; copy BPMN XML to compare.
Version history with diff view — Web Modeler tracks all saved versions and displays diffs between any two versions (BPMN element-level changes, attribute changes, removed/added elements).
Operate's process definition comparison.
No version history; the modeler only shows current.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Web Modeler's version history feature shows all versions chronologically and provides a diff view comparing any two versions — visual highlighting of added, removed, or changed BPMN elements.

(b) Workable but unnecessary: Web Modeler has built-in diff; external diff isn't needed.

(c) Incorrect: Web Modeler maintains version history.

(d) Incorrect: Operate doesn't have BPMN-element-level diff comparison; it's runtime monitoring.

a.text: Web Modeler has built-in diff; external diff isn't needed. — workable workaround but unneeded.

b.text: Web Modeler's version history feature shows all versions chronologically and provides a diff view comparing any two versions — visual highlighting of added, removed, or changed BPMN elements. — Version history + diff built-in.

c.text: Operate doesn't have BPMN-element-level diff comparison; it's runtime monitoring. — wrong feature scope.

d.text: Web Modeler maintains version history. — history exists.

scenario: A team manually starts a process instance via Operate (for testing). When prompted for initial variables, they must provide a JSON object. The variable schema requires `customer: {id: string, tier: string}`, `items: list of {sku, qty}`.
question: What is the correct JSON shape for these variables?

options.text: XML with variable name as attribute.

A flat JSON object: `{"customer": {"id": "C123", "tier": "GOLD"}, "items": [{"sku": "A1", "qty": 2}]}`. JSON object keys map to process variable names; nested objects and arrays are FEEL contexts and lists.
Newline-separated key-value pairs.
YAML with type declarations.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Camunda 8 process variables are JSON. Operate's manual-start UI takes a JSON object where each top-level key becomes a process variable. Nested objects and arrays map naturally to FEEL contexts and lists.

(b) Incorrect: Not the format.

(c) Incorrect: Not XML.

(d) Incorrect: Not YAML.

a.text: Not XML. — wrong format.

b.text: Camunda 8 process variables are JSON. Operate's manual-start UI takes a JSON object where each top-level key becomes a process variable. Nested objects and arrays map naturally to FEEL contexts and lists. — JSON canonical.

c.text: Not the format. — wrong format.

d.text: Not YAML. — wrong format.

scenario: A team must investigate why a `process-application` instance took 4 hours to complete when normal time is 30 minutes. They open Operate.
question: Which Operate views best aid this duration investigation?

options.text: Open the instance detail; review the execution timeline / flow node duration to identify which task took the longest. Operate shows per-task duration so a stuck or slow task is immediately visible.
Look at Optimize's process duration KPI — Operate doesn't have task-level timing.
Re-deploy the BPMN with debug enabled to capture timing.
Use the gRPC Zeebe API to dump raw events.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Operate's instance detail view shows the execution timeline with per-flow-node start/end timestamps and durations. For a slow instance, the slow task is immediately visible in the timeline — start with that.

(b) Misleading: Optimize aggregates KPIs across many instances; for a single slow instance, Operate's per-instance view is the right tool. Operate does have task-level timing.

(c) Wrong approach: No "debug mode" needed; timing is always recorded.

(d) Overkill: Raw gRPC events are deep diagnostic; Operate's UI is the right starting point.

a.text: Operate's instance detail view shows the execution timeline with per-flow-node start/end timestamps and durations. For a slow instance, the slow task is immediately visible in the timeline — start with that. — Operate instance detail timeline canonical.

b.text: Optimize aggregates KPIs across many instances; for a single slow instance, Operate's per-instance view is the right tool. Operate does have task-level timing. — wrong tool for single instance.

c.text: No "debug mode" needed; timing is always recorded. — invented mode.

d.text: Raw gRPC events are deep diagnostic; Operate's UI is the right starting point. — overkill.

scenario: A team accidentally started an instance of `dev-test-process` in production (wrong cluster). The instance is stuck waiting on a User Task that will never be completed because no one will work on it. They want to terminate this instance cleanly so it doesn't appear in active counts.
question: Which Operate action ends the instance?

options.text: Cancel process instance — terminates execution. Operate provides this action; the instance moves to "Cancelled" state. Historical record preserved.
Set retries to 0 on the User Task.
Delete the BPMN definition; instances auto-cancel.
Wait for the User Task due date; instance auto-fails.

explanation:
(a) Correct: "Cancel process instance" in Operate cleanly terminates the instance — Zeebe transitions it to Cancelled state. Historical record preserved in Operate for audit. The standard cleanup action.

(b) Wrong action: Deleting BPMN doesn't cancel running instances.

(c) Wrong mechanism: User Tasks don't have retries on job-completion semantics relevant here.

(d) Wrong action: User Task due dates surface as metadata, not auto-fail.

a.text: "Cancel process instance" in Operate cleanly terminates the instance — Zeebe transitions it to Cancelled state. Historical record preserved in Operate for audit. The standard cleanup action. — Cancel instance canonical termination.

b.text: User Tasks don't have retries on job-completion semantics relevant here. — wrong mechanism for User Tasks.

c.text: Deleting BPMN doesn't cancel running instances. — wrong action.

d.text: User Task due dates surface as metadata, not auto-fail. — due date ≠ auto-fail.

scenario: A developer wants to run a local Camunda 8 environment on their laptop for fast development iteration — Zeebe broker, Operate, Tasklist, Identity, Connectors. They want a single-binary setup that bundles everything, no Docker required.
question: Which Camunda 8 distribution fits this use case?

options.text: Camunda 8 SaaS local trial cluster.
Docker Compose with the full Camunda 8 stack.
Helm chart deployed to a local Kubernetes cluster (e.g., kind).

Camunda 8 Run — a single binary that bundles Zeebe + Operate + Tasklist + Identity + Connectors + Elasticsearch in one launchable package. Designed for local development and proof-of-concept work. Run with `./start.sh` (Linux/Mac) or `.\start.bat` (Windows).

explanation:
(a) Correct: Camunda 8 Run is purpose-built for fast local development: single binary, no Docker required, bundles all components, starts in seconds. It's the recommended "I want to try / iterate fast" distribution. Once started (typical ports: 8080 Operate, 8082 Tasklist, 26500 Zeebe gRPC), the developer can deploy processes immediately.

(b) Wrong scope: SaaS is cloud; the question is local.

(c) Workable but heavier: Docker Compose requires Docker; the question explicitly excludes Docker.

(d) Overkill: Helm + kind is a heavier setup for production-like local dev, not "fastest iteration."

a.text: SaaS is cloud; the question is local. — wrong scope (cloud).

b.text: Docker Compose requires Docker; the question explicitly excludes Docker. — workable but requires Docker.

c.text: Helm + kind is a heavier setup for production-like local dev, not "fastest iteration." — overkill fastest iteration.

d.text: Camunda 8 Run is purpose-built for fast local development: single binary, no Docker required, bundles all components, starts in seconds. It's the recommended "I want to try / iterate fast" distribution. Once started (typical ports: 8080 Operate, 8082 Tasklist, 26500 Zeebe gRPC), the developer can deploy processes immediately. — Camunda 8 Run single-binary local dev.

scenario: A logistics process tracks package handoff between three internal teams: Receiving, Sorting, Dispatch. Each team is a separate organisational unit but the process is one BPMN file. Management wants visual swim-lane separation so each team sees their own tasks immediately, while the overall flow remains a single process.
question: Which BPMN construct fits this requirement?

options.text: Single Pool with no Lanes; use Task documentation to indicate the team.
Three separate BPMN files chained via Call Activities.
Single Pool with three Lanes — one Lane per team. Tasks placed in their respective Lane visually segregate work; the process remains a single Zeebe-executable definition.
Three separate Pools — one per team — connected by Message Flows.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Lanes are the canonical BPMN construct for visual segregation within a single process. One Pool = one Zeebe process definition; multiple Lanes = visual ownership swim-lanes. Tasks placed in the Sorting Lane are clearly "Sorting's work" without breaking the single-process flow.

(b) Wrong scope: Multiple Pools represent separate participants that communicate via Message Flows — not internal teams of one organisation. Each Pool becomes its own process definition.

(c) Loses visibility: Documentation strings aren't visible without inspecting; defeats the "visual swim-lane separation" requirement.

(d) Over-engineered: Three BPMN files for internal team handoffs adds deployment/versioning overhead with no benefit.

a.text: Documentation strings aren't visible without inspecting; defeats the "visual swim-lane separation" requirement. — defeats visibility requirement.

b.text: Three BPMN files for internal team handoffs adds deployment/versioning overhead with no benefit. — over-engineered internal teams.

c.text: Lanes are the canonical BPMN construct for visual segregation within a single process. One Pool = one Zeebe process definition; multiple Lanes = visual ownership swim-lanes. Tasks placed in the Sorting Lane are clearly "Sorting's work" without breaking the single-process flow. — Lanes canonical visual segregation process definition.

d.text: Multiple Pools represent separate participants that communicate via Message Flows — not internal teams of one organisation. Each Pool becomes its own process definition. — wrong scope (Pools = different participants).

scenario: A customer-support ticket process must escalate to a supervisor if no agent picks up the User Task within 4 hours. The escalation should leave the original task active (a regular agent can still pick it up) but notify a supervisor in parallel.
question: Which BPMN construct best handles this?

options.text: Non-interrupting Timer Boundary Event attached to the User Task, configured with `PT4H`. When the timer fires, control branches to the supervisor-notify path while the original User Task stays active.
Event Subprocess with Message Start Event.
Add a parallel "wait 4 hours then notify" branch via Parallel Gateway.
Interrupting Timer Boundary Event with `PT4H`.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Non-interrupting Timer Boundary Event (dashed border) fires the timer without cancelling the host User Task. The supervisor-notify path runs in parallel while the original task remains active. This is canonical escalation-notify pattern.

(b) Wrong semantics: Interrupting (solid border) would cancel the User Task — agent can no longer pick it up.

(c) Wrong trigger: Event Subprocess fires on a different signal type (Message); Timer is the natural trigger for "after 4 hours."

(d) Works but ugly: Parallel Gateway with explicit wait is more verbose; Boundary Event is purpose-built.

a.text: Non-interrupting Timer Boundary Event (dashed border) fires the timer without cancelling the host User Task. The supervisor-notify path runs in parallel while the original task remains active. This is canonical escalation-notify pattern. — Non-interrupting Timer Boundary canonical parallel escalation.

b.text: Event Subprocess fires on a different signal type (Message); Timer is the natural trigger for "after 4 hours." — wrong trigger type for "4 hours."

c.text: Parallel Gateway with explicit wait is more verbose; Boundary Event is purpose-built. — works but verbose.

d.text: Interrupting (solid border) would cancel the User Task — agent can no longer pick it up. — cancels the original task (wrong semantics).

scenario: An e-commerce checkout has two alternative payment paths: card or PayPal. The user selects one at the start, and only that path executes. The two paths converge before the order-confirmation step.
question: Which gateway pair correctly models this?

options.text: Inclusive Gateway to split, Inclusive Gateway to join.
Event-Based Gateway to split, Exclusive Gateway to join.
Exclusive Gateway (XOR) to split, Exclusive Gateway (XOR) to join. Only the selected branch executes; the join waits for one token.
Parallel Gateway to split, Parallel Gateway to join.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Exclusive Gateway (XOR) selects exactly one outgoing path based on condition (e.g., `=paymentMethod = "card"`). The joining XOR merges any single token without waiting for others. Canonical "either/or" branching.

(b) Wrong semantics: Parallel Gateway runs both paths — would charge the customer twice.

(c) Overkill: Inclusive Gateway allows one or many paths; XOR is sufficient and clearer for strict either/or.

(d) Wrong trigger: Event-Based Gateway routes on incoming events (Messages, Timers), not on a process variable. Used for "wait for whichever arrives first" scenarios.

a.text: Inclusive Gateway allows one or many paths; XOR is sufficient and clearer for strict either/or. — works overkill.

b.text: Event-Based Gateway routes on incoming events (Messages, Timers), not on a process variable. Used for "wait for whichever arrives first" scenarios. — wrong trigger (events, not variable).

c.text: Exclusive Gateway (XOR) selects exactly one outgoing path based on condition (e.g., `=paymentMethod = "card"`). The joining XOR merges any single token without waiting for others. Canonical "either/or" branching. — XOR–XOR canonical either/or.

d.text: Parallel Gateway runs both paths — would charge the customer twice.

scenario: A travel-booking process must invoke a separately-maintained `payment-processing` BPMN as part of its flow. The payment-processing BPMN is owned by a different team, has its own lifecycle, and is reused by 5 other processes.
question: Which BPMN construct invokes another deployed process synchronously?

options.text: Service Task with `type = "call-process"`.
Embedded Subprocess containing the payment-processing logic copied inline.
Send Task that delivers a Message Start trigger.
Call Activity referencing the called process definition by its `processId`. The parent process pauses until the called process completes; data is passed in/out via input/output mappings.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Call Activity is the canonical BPMN construct for synchronous invocation of another deployed process. The parent suspends, the called process executes, results return via output mapping, parent resumes. Independent lifecycle of the called definition is preserved — perfect for shared, reusable subprocesses.

(b) Wrong semantics: Send Task + Message Start triggers an asynchronous new instance; no automatic return-flow.

(c) Defeats reuse: Embedded copy means 6 places to maintain the same logic.

(d) Invented type: No `call-process` Service Task type; Call Activity is its own BPMN element.

a.text: No `call-process` Service Task type; Call Activity is its own BPMN element. — invented mechanism.

b.text: Embedded copy means 6 places to maintain the same logic. — defeats reuse (6× maintenance).

c.text: Send Task + Message Start triggers an asynchronous new instance; no automatic return-flow. — async; no return-flow.

d.text: Call Activity is the canonical BPMN construct for synchronous invocation of another deployed process. The parent suspends, the called process executes, results return via output mapping, parent resumes. Independent lifecycle of the called definition is preserved — perfect for shared, reusable subprocesses. — Call Activity canonical synchronous reuse.

scenario: A risk-assessment process runs for each customer in a list. The customer list comes from a previous step. The team wants all customers assessed in parallel to minimise total processing time. Each assessment produces a `score`; all scores are collected at the end.
question: Which BPMN construct fits?

options.text: Loop with Exclusive Gateway iterating through customers.
Single Service Task with FEEL `for customer in customers return assess(customer)`.

Parallel Multi-Instance Subprocess with `inputCollection = customers`, `inputElement = customer`, `outputCollection = scores`, `outputElement = score`. All instances execute concurrently; scores accumulate into a list.
Sequential Multi-Instance Subprocess.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Parallel Multi-Instance executes one instance per `inputCollection` element concurrently. `outputElement` per instance accumulates into `outputCollection`. Designed exactly for "for each X in parallel + collect results."

(b) Wrong execution mode: Sequential runs instances one-after-another — slower; question says "in parallel."

(c) No native loop: BPMN doesn't have a loop gateway construct; Multi-Instance is the proper iteration pattern.

(d) Wrong place for logic: Stuffing iteration into FEEL inside one Service Task hides what BPMN should expose visually; also limits per-instance error handling, retries, etc.

a.text: BPMN doesn't have a loop gateway construct; Multi-Instance is the proper iteration pattern. — BPMN loop gateway.

b.text: Stuffing iteration into FEEL inside one Service Task hides what BPMN should expose visually; also limits per-instance error handling, retries, etc. — hides iteration, lacks per-instance lifecycle.

c.text: Parallel Multi-Instance executes one instance per `inputCollection` element concurrently. `outputElement` per instance accumulates into `outputCollection`. Designed exactly for "for each X in parallel + collect results." — Parallel MI + inputCollection/outputCollection canonical.

d.text: Sequential runs instances one-after-another — slower; question says "in parallel." — sequential, parallel.

scenario: A loan-application process must wait for either a credit-check response message OR a 48-hour timeout — whichever arrives first. Only one path should execute.
question: Which BPMN construct fits?

options.text: Exclusive Gateway with FEEL condition checking which event arrived.
Two parallel Boundary Events on a placeholder User Task.
Parallel Gateway; downstream Exclusive picks.
Event-Based Gateway with two outgoing paths: one to Intermediate Message Catch Event (credit-check), one to Intermediate Timer Catch Event (PT48H). First event wins; the other is cancelled.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Event-Based Gateway is purpose-built for "first event wins" pattern. Process pauses at the gateway; downstream Intermediate Catch Events compete; first triggered event proceeds, others are cancelled atomically. This is the canonical race-condition construct.

(b) Wrong direction: Exclusive Gateway evaluates data conditions, not waits for events.

(c) Wrong semantics: Parallel runs all branches; doesn't model "first wins."

(d) Workaround, not canonical: Boundary Events on a placeholder task technically works but the placeholder pollutes the diagram; Event-Based Gateway is purpose-built.

a.text: Exclusive Gateway evaluates data conditions, not waits for events.

b.text: Boundary Events on a placeholder task technically works but the placeholder pollutes the diagram; Event-Based Gateway is purpose-built. — workaround.

c.text: Parallel runs all branches; doesn't model "first wins." — Parallel runs all.

d.text: Event-Based Gateway is purpose-built for "first event wins" pattern. Process pauses at the gateway; downstream Intermediate Catch Events compete; first triggered event proceeds, others are cancelled atomically. This is the canonical race-condition construct. — Event-Based Gateway canonical race construct.

scenario: A claim-processing diagram has 12 tasks. The handle exceptions logic (notify ops, log incident, route to manual review) should be modelled in a separate visual area that can fire whenever a specific error message arrives during the main flow.
question: Which BPMN construct fits?

options.text: Event Subprocess with Message Start Event inside the parent process. The Event Subprocess sits visually separate, listens for the message, and runs without disturbing the main flow (non-interrupting) or by replacing it (interrupting).
Boundary Message Event on each of the 12 tasks.
Add the exception logic at the end of the main flow with conditional gateway.
Separate BPMN file invoked by Call Activity.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Event Subprocess is the canonical BPMN construct for scoped event handling within a process. It sits inside the parent (visually separate area), listens for its trigger (Message Start), and activates anytime during the parent's execution. Cleaner than 12 boundary events.

(b) Different scope: Call Activity calls another deployed process — separate definition, separate lifecycle; doesn't share the parent's variable scope as cleanly.

(c) Wrong placement: "At the end" doesn't fire during the main flow when needed.

(d) Diagram pollution: 12 boundary events × 12 tasks = visual mess; Event Subprocess is the DRY solution.

a.text: Event Subprocess is the canonical BPMN construct for scoped event handling within a process. It sits inside the parent (visually separate area), listens for its trigger (Message Start), and activates anytime during the parent's execution. Cleaner than 12 boundary events. — Event Subprocess canonical scoped event handler.

b.text: 12 boundary events × 12 tasks = visual mess; Event Subprocess is the DRY solution. — diagram pollution.

c.text: "At the end" doesn't fire during the main flow when needed. — wrong placement (end-only).

d.text: Call Activity calls another deployed process — separate definition, separate lifecycle; doesn't share the parent's variable scope as cleanly. — wrong scope (separate definition).

scenario: A manual quality-review process consists of three steps that a human reviewer performs in any order they prefer: visual inspection, document check, sample test. All three must complete before the process continues, but the order is not constrained.
question: Which BPMN construct fits?

options.text: Parallel Gateway split with three User Tasks, Parallel Gateway join.
Three User Tasks in sequence with conditional skipping.
Sequential Multi-Instance over a list of step types.
Ad-hoc Subprocess with three User Tasks inside. Reviewer picks which to perform when; the Ad-hoc subprocess completes when all are done (or per completionCondition).

explanation:
(a) Correct: Ad-hoc Subprocess is the BPMN construct for unordered tasks — performer chooses when/which to do. All inner activities are available; completion is governed by `completionCondition`. Designed for human-driven flexible flows.

(b) Imposes structure: Parallel Gateway implies all three start simultaneously and run in parallel; in practice a human does them serially but the model misrepresents that they're flexibly-ordered.

(c) Wrong fit: Multi-Instance iterates over a collection of similar work; here the three tasks are distinct.

(d) Imposes order: Sequence implies order; the requirement is "in any order."

a.text: Parallel Gateway implies all three start simultaneously and run in parallel; in practice a human does them serially but the model misrepresents that they're flexibly-ordered. — works practically but misrepresents semantics.

b.text: Sequence implies order; the requirement is "in any order." — imposes order.

c.text: Multi-Instance iterates over a collection of similar work; here the three tasks are distinct. — wrong fit (iteration over similar items).

d.text: Ad-hoc Subprocess is the BPMN construct for unordered tasks — performer chooses when/which to do. All inner activities are available; completion is governed by `completionCondition`. Designed for human-driven flexible flows. — Ad-hoc Subprocess canonical unordered tasks.

scenario: A purchase-order process has a `place-order` Service Task and a `charge-card` Service Task. If `charge-card` fails after the order is placed, the team wants to automatically cancel the placed order as part of failure recovery.
question: Which BPMN construct fits compensation semantics?

options.text: Error Boundary Event on `charge-card`.
Conditional Sequence Flow with FEEL `=chargeStatus != "OK"`.
Add an explicit `cancel-order` Service Task on the failure path.

Compensation Event — attach a Compensation Boundary Event to `place-order` with an associated Compensation Handler (`cancel-order` Service Task). When `charge-card` fires a Compensation End Event or Compensation Throwing Event triggers, BPMN walks back through completed activities and executes their handlers.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Compensation is BPMN's purpose-built mechanism for undoing completed work in saga-style transactions. Attach Compensation Boundary Event to the activity whose effect needs undoing (`place-order`), wire the handler (`cancel-order`). When charge-card fails and throws a Compensation Event, BPMN walks completed activities in reverse and runs each handler. Cleaner than manual recovery paths.

(b) Different semantics: Error Boundary handles errors on the failing task, not undo logic for prior tasks.

(c) Misses the undo: A conditional flow can route to a manual recovery path but doesn't express compensation semantics.

(d) Works but manual: Explicit recovery paths get unwieldy when there are many completed steps to roll back.

a.text: Error Boundary handles errors on the failing task, not undo logic for prior tasks. — different scope (failing task error).

b.text: A conditional flow can route to a manual recovery path but doesn't express compensation semantics. — no compensation semantics.

c.text: Explicit recovery paths get unwieldy when there are many completed steps to roll back. — works but doesn't scale.

d.text: Compensation is BPMN's purpose-built mechanism for undoing completed work in saga-style transactions. Attach Compensation Boundary Event to the activity whose effect needs undoing (`place-order`), wire the handler (`cancel-order`). When charge-card fails and throws a Compensation Event, BPMN walks completed activities in reverse and runs each handler. Cleaner than manual recovery paths. — Compensation canonical saga undo.

scenario: A Service Task is configured to call a partner-credit-check service. In Web Modeler, the modeler must specify which Job Worker handles it. The team has a worker registered with type `credit-check-v2`.
question: Which Service Task property binds the BPMN to that worker?

options.text: `name` attribute on the BPMN element — workers match by task name.
`processId` of the BPMN file.
`id` attribute of the BPMN element.

`zeebe:taskDefinition type="credit-check-v2"` — set via the Implementation → Job Type property in Web Modeler. Workers subscribe to this exact string; Zeebe routes activated jobs to subscribed workers.

explanation:
(a) Correct: `zeebe:taskDefinition` with `type` attribute is the canonical Zeebe extension. Workers subscribe to this type string; Zeebe routes jobs accordingly. Set via the Implementation property panel.

(b) Incorrect: `name` is display-only.

(c) Incorrect: `processId` identifies the process, not task binding.

(d) Incorrect: `id` is internal element ID; workers don't match on it.

a.text: `name` is display-only. — name = display.

b.text: `processId` identifies the process, not task binding. — wrong scope.

c.text: `id` is internal element ID; workers don't match on it. — wrong attribute.

d.text: `zeebe:taskDefinition` with `type` attribute is the canonical Zeebe extension. Workers subscribe to this type string; Zeebe routes jobs accordingly. Set via the Implementation property panel. — zeebe:taskDefinition type canonical binding.

scenario: An order-routing BPMN has an Exclusive Gateway splitting on order value: `> 10000 → review`, `<= 10000 → auto-approve`. The modeler writes the condition expression on the sequence flow.
question: Which FEEL expression is correct for the "review" branch?

options.text: `${order.value > 10000}` — JUEL syntax from Camunda 7.
`order.value > 10000` (no prefix).
`=order.value > 10000` — FEEL expression prefixed with `=`, evaluating against the process variable `order.value`.
`#{order.value > 10000}` — Spring EL.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Camunda 8 uses FEEL with `=` prefix marker. The expression evaluates to boolean; sequence flow taken when true.

(b) Camunda 7 reflex: JUEL `${}` is C7 syntax, not C8.

(c) Missing prefix: Without `=` it's treated as plain text, not an expression.

(d) Wrong language: Spring EL is not C8 FEEL.

a.text: JUEL `${}` is C7 syntax, not C8. — C7 reflex.

b.text: Without `=` it's treated as plain text, not an expression. — missing prefix.

c.text: Camunda 8 uses FEEL with `=` prefix marker. The expression evaluates to boolean; sequence flow taken when true. — `=` FEEL prefix.

d.text: Spring EL is not C8 FEEL. — wrong language.

scenario: A User Task in a claims process must be assigned to the user whose ID is stored in `claim.assignedAgent`. The team configures the User Task in Web Modeler.
question: Which configuration assigns the User Task dynamically?

options.text: Hardcode the user ID in the BPMN.
Use Task Listener "on-create" to set assignee programmatically.
Cannot dynamically assign; must use claim.
Set `zeebe:assignmentDefinition assignee="=claim.assignedAgent"` — FEEL expression evaluated at activation; result becomes the assignee for that task instance.

explanation:
(a) Correct: `zeebe:assignmentDefinition` accepts FEEL expressions in `assignee` and `candidateGroups`. Evaluated at activation; result is set as the User Task's assignee.

(b) Different concept: Task Listeners aren't a Camunda 8 concept the same way as C7; assignmentDefinition handles dynamic assignment natively.

(c) Defeats requirement: Hardcoded contradicts "dynamically."

(d) Incorrect: Dynamic assignment is supported via FEEL.

a.text: Hardcoded contradicts "dynamically." — contradicts requirement.

b.text: Task Listeners aren't a Camunda 8 concept the same way as C7; assignmentDefinition handles dynamic assignment natively. — C7 reflex.

c.text: Dynamic assignment is supported via FEEL.

d.text: `zeebe:assignmentDefinition` accepts FEEL expressions in `assignee` and `candidateGroups`. Evaluated at activation; result is set as the User Task's assignee. — assignmentDefinition + FEEL.

scenario: A Business Rule Task in a pricing process must invoke a deployed DMN decision with the ID `discount-calculator` and return the result into variable `discountPct`.
question: Which configuration is correct?

options.text: `zeebe:calledDecision decisionId="discount-calculator" resultVariable="discountPct"`.
Call Activity referencing the DMN file.
Service Task with FEEL `=invoke("discount-calculator")`.
`zeebe:taskDefinition type="dmn-eval"` and pass decision ID as header.

explanation:
(a) Correct: `zeebe:calledDecision` extension on Business Rule Task is the canonical native binding. Specifies `decisionId` and `resultVariable`; Zeebe evaluates the DMN inline (no separate worker needed).

(b) Wrong approach: Service-Task-with-DMN-worker is C7 reflex; C8 has native binding.

(c) Wrong construct: Call Activity invokes BPMN processes, not DMN decisions.

(d) Invented function: No `invoke()` FEEL built-in.

a.text: `zeebe:calledDecision` extension on Business Rule Task is the canonical native binding. Specifies `decisionId` and `resultVariable`; Zeebe evaluates the DMN inline (no separate worker needed). — zeebe:calledDecision native binding.

b.text: Call Activity invokes BPMN processes, not DMN decisions. — wrong construct.

c.text: No `invoke()` FEEL built-in. — invented function.

d.text: Service-Task-with-DMN-worker is C7 reflex; C8 has native binding. — C7 reflex (worker for DMN).

scenario: A Service Task expects an `orderId` input. The process variable carrying this is `currentOrder.id` (nested). The modeler wants to map `currentOrder.id` to the local variable `orderId` available only inside the task.
question: Which configuration achieves this?

options.text: Use a Script Task before to set `orderId` globally.
Pass full `currentOrder` to the worker; worker extracts `id`.
Rename the process variable globally to `orderId`.
Input mapping on the Service Task — Target: `orderId`, Source expression: `=currentOrder.id`.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Input mappings on a task scope local variables to that task's execution. `Target: orderId, Source: =currentOrder.id` extracts the nested value and exposes it as `orderId` within the Service Task. Process scope unchanged.

(b) Bigger payload: Sending full nested object is workable but exposes more than needed (LSP).

(c) Side effects: Renaming globally affects all consumers.

(d) Pollutes process scope: Globally-set `orderId` leaks beyond this task.

a.text: Globally-set `orderId` leaks beyond this task. — pollutes process scope.

b.text: Sending full nested object is workable but exposes more than needed (LSP). — works over-shares.

c.text: Renaming globally affects all consumers. — global side effects.

d.text: Input mappings on a task scope local variables to that task's execution. `Target: orderId, Source: =currentOrder.id` extracts the nested value and exposes it as `orderId` within the Service Task. Process scope unchanged. — Input mapping = local scoping.

scenario: A claims process must store uploaded photos and PDFs (5-50 MB each) as part of the process state. Storing them as base64 process variables would bloat the broker state.
question: Which Camunda 8.6+ feature handles this cleanly?

options.text: Base64-encode and store as a process variable.
Document Handling — upload via the Documents API; receive a document reference; pass the reference as a process variable. Storage is configurable (in-memory dev, S3 prod, etc.).
Store outside Camunda; pass only URL in variables.
Camunda 8 doesn't support binary attachments; reject the requirement.

(a) Correct: Camunda 8.6+ Document Handling is the first-class document-as-variable feature. Documents API stores binary content in configured storage backend; the process variable holds a reference. Downstream tasks/Connectors fetch the document via the reference when needed.

(b) Anti-pattern: Base64 in variables bloats broker state, hurts performance.

(c) Workable but lacks integration: Plain URL works loses Camunda-managed lifecycle, TTL, security integration.

(d) Incorrect: Document Handling is the answer.

a.text: Base64 in variables bloats broker state, hurts performance. — bloats state.

b.text: Camunda 8.6+ Document Handling is the first-class document-as-variable feature. Documents API stores binary content in configured storage backend; the process variable holds a reference. Downstream tasks/Connectors fetch the document via the reference when needed. — Document Handling canonical.

c.text: Plain URL works loses Camunda-managed lifecycle, TTL, security integration. — works lacks integration.

d.text: Document Handling is the answer.

scenario: A Sequential Multi-Instance Subprocess iterates over `customers`. Each instance does account-validation. The team wants only one instance at a time because the back-end API has a 1-request-per-second limit.
question: Which Multi-Instance configuration fits?

options.text: Multi-Instance can't be rate-limited; use a Queue.
Sequential Multi-Instance with `inputCollection = customers`, `inputElement = customer`. Sequential mode runs one instance at a time — fits the rate-limit constraint naturally.
Parallel Multi-Instance with `loopCardinality = 1`.
Parallel Multi-Instance and add `sleep(1)` in the worker.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Sequential mode runs instances one at a time — natural fit for serialised work. The 1-RPS limit is satisfied if each instance completes in ≥ 1 sec.

(b) Wrong semantics: loopCardinality = 1 means one instance total, not rate-limited.

(c) Worker-side hack: Combining Parallel with worker sleeps creates fragile timing; sequential mode is canonical.

(d) Incorrect: Sequential mode addresses this natively.

a.text: Sequential mode addresses this natively. — Sequential handles it.

b.text: Sequential mode runs instances one at a time — natural fit for serialised work. The 1-RPS limit is satisfied if each instance completes in ≥ 1 sec. — Sequential MI = canonical serialisation.

c.text: loopCardinality = 1 means one instance total, not rate-limited. — wrong semantics.

d.text: Combining Parallel with worker sleeps creates fragile timing; sequential mode is canonical. — fragile hack.

scenario: A bank-onboarding process uses IDP (Intelligent Document Processing) to extract structured data from uploaded passport scans. The IDP extraction is configured in Web Modeler. After extraction, the next Service Task needs the extracted `passport.surname`.

options.text: Cannot access in FEEL; a Service Task must call IDP API to retrieve.
IDP returns plaintext; parse it in a Script Task.
IDP results are stored externally; the process gets only a key.
IDP Application result is exposed as a process variable with structured fields (e.g., `idpResult.passport.surname`). FEEL navigates the result like any nested object.

(b) Wrong model: Results are first-class process variables, not externally keyed.

(c) Wrong abstraction: Native integration eliminates manual API calls.

(d) Incorrect: Structured fields, not plaintext.

a.text: Native integration eliminates manual API calls. — wrong abstraction.

b.text: Structured fields, not plaintext.

c.text: Results are first-class process variables, not externally keyed. — wrong model.

d.text: IDP Applications expose extraction results as structured process variables. The result object is FEEL-navigable. Modeler defines extraction schema; runtime populates the variable. — Structured FEEL-navigable variable.

scenario: A team builds a custom REST Service Task that wraps the corporate API. Every team in the company uses it. The modeling group wants a single source of truth for its configuration: URL prefix, headers, retry policy. Different processes can override only the endpoint path.

options.text: Element Template published at organisation scope. Defines the Service Task's properties with defaults and constraints; modelers in any project apply it and override allowed fields only.
Process-level start variable with all config.
Custom Connector with hardcoded prefix.
Shared BPMN library with reusable subprocesses.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Element Templates at org scope provide DRY task definitions with sensible defaults and field-level overrides. Web Modeler offers them in the template picker; modelers select and customise allowed fields only.

(b) Heavier alternative: Custom Connector works but requires SDK code; Element Template is config-only.

(c) Wrong scope: Subprocess library handles BPMN reuse, not task-config reuse.

(d) Wrong scope: Start variables aren't reusable across processes.

a.text: Element Templates at org scope provide DRY task definitions with sensible defaults and field-level overrides. Web Modeler offers them in the template picker; modelers select and customise allowed fields only. — Element Template + org scope.

b.text: Start variables aren't reusable across processes. — wrong scope.

c.text: Custom Connector works but requires SDK code; Element Template is config-only. — heavier alternative.

d.text: Subprocess library handles BPMN reuse, not task-config reuse. — wrong scope.

scenario: A customer-service process needs an LLM-driven agent to autonomously decide a sequence of tool calls — query CRM, summarise tickets, recommend response — based on conversation context. The team wants Camunda 8 to orchestrate the agent's tool selection without modelling each step.

options.text: Sequence of explicit Service Tasks; team picks the order.
Custom RPA bot.

AI Agent Connector with Ad-hoc Subprocess — Ad-hoc contains the available tools (Service Tasks for CRM query, summarisation, recommendation). AI Agent Connector calls the LLM, gets a tool plan, executes via ad-hoc, loops until completion.
Single Service Task with a long FEEL expression branching the work.

(b) Wrong abstraction: FEEL doesn't drive LLM tool-selection.

(c) Defeats dynamism: Explicit sequence prescribes order; agent must choose.

(d) Wrong feature: RPA drives UIs, not LLM agents.

b.text: RPA drives UIs, not LLM agents. — RPA ≠ LLM agent.

c.text: Camunda 8.8 introduces Agentic Orchestration via AI Agent Connector + Ad-hoc Subprocess. The LLM decides tools dynamically; Ad-hoc Subprocess hosts the available tools; the loop continues until the LLM signals completion. Purpose-built for autonomous agents. — AI Agent + Ad-hoc canonical agentic pattern.

d.text: FEEL doesn't drive LLM tool-selection. — wrong abstraction.

(c) Manual: Doesn't scale.

(d) Incorrect: TTL is supported.

a.text: TTL is supported.

b.text: Doesn't scale. — manual.

c.text: Cron + API works but reinvents what TTL provides. — reinvent wheel.

scenario: A subscription-renewal process has a periodic check every 1st of the month at midnight: "Has the customer's payment method expired?" The check should run for the duration of an active subscription (typically 12 months).

Cron expression `0 0 0 1 * *`.
ISO 8601 cycle `R12/2026-01-01T00:00:00Z/P1M` — repeats 12 times, anchored at the next Jan 1st midnight UTC, with 1-month period.
Duration `P1M` with manual re-entry.

(d) Correct: Camunda 8 Timer Cycle accepts both ISO 8601 repeating intervals (`R12/.../P1M`) and cron expressions for more complex scheduling needs. Both are documented and supported.

scenario: A KYC verification process has a mandatory ESCALATE path when an automated check returns "suspicious." The team wants to leave the main flow continuing (parallel audit), but immediately throw a non-blocking escalation upward to a parent enclosing scope (Event Subprocess in parent).
question: Which BPMN event fires this?

options.text: Send Task with message to parent.

Throw Escalation Intermediate Event — propagates an escalation code upward without stopping the current flow. Parent Event Subprocess with matching Escalation Start catches it. Non-interrupting by default for parent matching.
Signal Throw Event.
Throw Error End Event.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Escalation Throw Intermediate Event propagates an escalation code upward in scope without interrupting the throwing flow. Matched by an Escalation Catch (Boundary or Start in Event Subprocess) in the enclosing scope. Designed for non-blocking notification of higher-level handlers.

(b) Wrong semantics: Error End terminates the throwing flow and is for errors not escalations.

(c) Wrong scope: Messages are inter-process, not intra-process scope propagation.

(d) Broadcast: Signal is broadcast to all listeners across processes; Escalation is scope-local.

a.text: Messages are inter-process, not intra-process scope propagation. — inter-process not scope.

b.text: Escalation Throw Intermediate Event propagates an escalation code upward in scope without interrupting the throwing flow. Matched by an Escalation Catch (Boundary or Start in Event Subprocess) in the enclosing scope. Designed for non-blocking notification of higher-level handlers. — Escalation Throw + non-blocking parent catch.

c.text: Signal is broadcast to all listeners across processes; Escalation is scope-local. — broadcast scope.

d.text: Error End terminates the throwing flow and is for errors not escalations. — terminates flow.

scenario: A pricing decision table determines discount per customer. The team wants to ensure that only one rule matches for any given input — if two rules ever match, the deployment should fail (model-validation-time guard).
question: Which hit policy provides this guarantee?

options.text: FIRST.
ANY.

UNIQUE hit policy. At runtime, if more than one rule matches the same input, an error is raised (UNIQUE policy contract is "exactly one rule must match"). Modelers must design rules to be mutually exclusive.
COLLECT.

explanation:
(a) Correct: UNIQUE mandates exactly one matching rule per evaluation; multiple matches throw an error. Used when rules should be mutually exclusive and overlap is a defect.

(b) Different semantics: ANY allows multiple rules to match as long as they produce identical output — different intent.

(c) Different semantics: FIRST returns the first match in row order; multiple matches don't error.

(d) Different semantics: COLLECT gathers all matching outputs into a list.

a.text: FIRST returns the first match in row order; multiple matches don't error. — different (first match wins).

b.text: ANY allows multiple rules to match as long as they produce identical output — different intent. — different (identical outputs allowed).

c.text: UNIQUE mandates exactly one matching rule per evaluation; multiple matches throw an error. Used when rules should be mutually exclusive and overlap is a defect. — UNIQUE = exactly one match contract.

d.text: COLLECT gathers all matching outputs into a list. — different (collects all).

scenario: An insurance-rate decision table has 8 rules. The team wants the result to be the sum of all matching rules' premium amounts (additive pricing model).
question: Which hit policy + aggregator fits?

options.text: COLLECT with aggregator SUM. All matching rules' outputs are summed; result is a single number.
RULE ORDER.
COLLECT without aggregator.
PRIORITY by output priority.

explanation:
(a) Correct: COLLECT with SUM aggregator returns the sum of all matching rules' output values. Designed for additive aggregation scenarios.

(b) Wrong result type: COLLECT without aggregator returns a list; the question asks for a sum.

(c) Different semantics: PRIORITY picks one based on priority order.

(d) Different semantics: RULE ORDER returns a list ordered by rule order.

a.text: COLLECT with SUM aggregator returns the sum of all matching rules' output values. Designed for additive aggregation scenarios. — COLLECT+SUM canonical.

b.text: RULE ORDER returns a list ordered by rule order. — list, not sum.

c.text: COLLECT without aggregator returns a list; the question asks for a sum. — list, not sum.

d.text: PRIORITY picks one based on priority order. — picks one, not aggregates.

scenario: A loan-eligibility DMN has 10 detailed rules. The team wants the engine to evaluate rules in priority order (output preference: PREMIUM > GOLD > SILVER > BRONZE) and return the single highest-priority match.
question: Which hit policy fits?

options.text: PRIORITY — rules evaluated; among matching rules, the output with highest output priority (defined in output value list ordering) wins. Returns single value.
OUTPUT ORDER — returns list ordered by output priority.
FIRST.
UNIQUE.

explanation:
(a) Correct: PRIORITY policy: among matching rules, the engine picks the one whose output value has the highest priority per the output's value-list ordering. Returns a single value. Used when multiple rules can match but output preference dominates.

(b) Row-order based: FIRST returns first-matching by row, not by output priority.

(c) Different intent: UNIQUE requires exactly one match.

(d) Returns list: OUTPUT ORDER orders all matches into a list; PRIORITY returns single value.

(b) Wrong syntax: `=>` is invalid.

(c) Wrong type: Numeric comparison with string literal fails type check.

(d) Invented function: FEEL doesn't have `gt()`.

scenario: A diagnostic DMN has a Decision Requirements Diagram (DRD) with 3 chained decisions: `Symptom Analysis` → `Diagnosis Group` → `Recommended Treatment`. The Treatment decision depends on Diagnosis Group's output.
question: How is this dependency expressed in DMN?

options.text: Information Requirement arrow from `Diagnosis Group` (as the supplier) to `Recommended Treatment` (as the consumer). Indicates Treatment requires Diagnosis output.
Knowledge Requirement arrow.
Direct invocation in FEEL `=invoke("DiagnosisGroup")`.
Authority Requirement.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Information Requirement (solid arrow) is the DRD connector expressing "this decision uses the output of that decision." Standard DMN dependency.

(b) Different semantics: Knowledge Requirement (dashed) is for Business Knowledge Models (reusable decision logic), not decision-to-decision flow.

(c) Different semantics: Authority Requirement (dashed circle) expresses "this knowledge source authorises this decision" — governance, not data flow.

(d) Wrong abstraction: Decisions chain via DRD structure, not FEEL invocation.

b.text: Knowledge Requirement (dashed) is for Business Knowledge Models (reusable decision logic), not decision-to-decision flow. — different (BKM).

c.text: Decisions chain via DRD structure, not FEEL invocation. — wrong abstraction.

d.text: Authority Requirement (dashed circle) expresses "this knowledge source authorises this decision" — governance, not data flow. — different (governance).

scenario: A DMN decision table has a numeric output column for `discount`. The modeler wants to express "if amount is between 100 and 1000, output 10%" — using a single rule row.

options.text: `>= 100, <= 1000` (comma-separated).

explanation:
(a) Correct: FEEL range syntax `[a..b]` denotes inclusive-inclusive. Other forms: `(a..b)` exclusive-exclusive, `[a..b)` half-open.

(b) Missing brackets: Plain `100..1000` is not the range literal; needs `[..]` markers.

(c) Wrong syntax: No `between` keyword in FEEL.

(d) Different semantics: Comma in unary tests is OR, not AND — `>= 100, <= 1000` matches either condition, not both.

b.text: No `between` keyword in FEEL. — invented keyword.

c.text: Plain `100..1000` is not the range literal; needs `[..]` markers. — missing brackets.

d.text: FEEL range syntax `[a..b]` denotes inclusive-inclusive. Other forms: `(a..b)` exclusive-exclusive, `[a..b)` half-open. — [100..1000] inclusive range.

scenario: A medical-intake form has a Radio field "Pregnant?" (yes/no). The follow-up field "Weeks of pregnancy" should appear only if the user selected "yes." The team wants no JavaScript — pure declarative.

(b) Heavier: Two forms duplicate fields; conditional is DRY.

(c) Wrong tool: CSS hides the DOM element but the field is still rendered/validated; conditional removes it from form lifecycle.

scenario: An expense-claim form contains a list of expense rows — each row has `category`, `amount`, `receiptUrl`. The user can add/remove rows. After submit, the process variable `expenses` should be a list of objects.

scenario: An Outbound HTTP Connector calls a partner API that returns paginated results (1000 items per page; 50 pages total). The team wants to fetch all 50 pages without manually modelling 50 calls.
question: Which BPMN+Connector pattern handles paginated fetch?

options.text: Cannot paginate; fetch only the first page.
Single Connector call with `paginate = true`.
Custom Connector built specifically for this.

(a) Correct: Pagination is a BPMN-level pattern: loop the Connector call within MI Subprocess (sequential when tokens chain, parallel when pages are independent), accumulate via outputCollection.

(b) Invented flag: HTTP Connector doesn't have a `paginate` toggle.

(c) Over-engineered: Standard Connector + BPMN loop suffices.

(d) Incorrect: Pagination is solvable via BPMN constructs.

b.text: HTTP Connector doesn't have a `paginate` toggle. — invented flag.

c.text: Standard Connector + BPMN loop suffices. — over-engineered.

d.text: Pagination is a BPMN-level pattern: loop the Connector call within MI Subprocess (sequential when tokens chain, parallel when pages are independent), accumulate via outputCollection. — MI loop + outputCollection.

scenario: An Inbound Webhook Connector receives external system callbacks. To prevent unauthorized calls, the team wants to require an HMAC signature in the request, validated by the Connector before correlation.

options.text: Webhook Connectors are public by design; can't authenticate.

(b) Too late: By the time the Service Task runs, the instance has been created — invalid callbacks have already polluted state.

(c) Incorrect: Webhook supports multiple auth methods.

(d) Heavier than needed: Proxy/mTLS works but HMAC is the canonical Webhook-level mechanism.

b.text: By the time the Service Task runs, the instance has been created — invalid callbacks have already polluted state. — too late (instance already created).

c.text: Proxy/mTLS works but HMAC is the canonical Webhook-level mechanism. — heavier alternative.

d.text: Webhook Connector supports HMAC signature validation natively. Configure algorithm + secret + header; Connector verifies before instance creation. — Native HMAC validation.

scenario: An Outbound HTTP Connector calls a partner API. The partner enforces a 30-second timeout. The team wants the Connector call to time out client-side at 25 seconds (before partner times out) so the process can retry or fall back cleanly.

(b) Wrong scope: Job-activation timeout governs how long the worker holds the job, not per-call HTTP timeout.

(c) Workable BPMN-level: Timer Boundary cancels the task on timeout but doesn't gracefully close the HTTP request; Connector-level timeout is cleaner.

d.text: Job-activation timeout governs how long the worker holds the job, not per-call HTTP timeout. — wrong scope.

scenario: A Java Job Worker built with Spring Zeebe handles `payment-validation` Service Tasks. The worker is annotated `@JobWorker(type = "payment-validation", autoComplete = false)`. The team is unsure of the implication of `autoComplete = false`.
question: What does `autoComplete = false` mean?

options.text: The worker is read-only; cannot modify variables.
The job is never completed; the instance hangs.
The worker auto-fails on every invocation.

The worker is responsible for explicitly calling `jobClient.newCompleteCommand(...).send()` (or fail/throw) — Spring Zeebe will not auto-complete after the method returns. Useful when the worker wants async completion or to return after starting background work.

explanation:
(a) Correct: `autoComplete = false` tells Spring Zeebe not to auto-complete the job after the handler returns. The handler takes responsibility for completion. Common use case: async work where the handler delegates to a thread pool / external system and completes the job later.

(b) Misleading: Hangs only if handler doesn't complete; not automatic hang.

(c) Incorrect: Variable modification is independent.

(d) Incorrect: No auto-fail behavior.

a.text: Variable modification is independent.

b.text: Hangs only if handler doesn't complete; not automatic hang. — misleading.

c.text: No auto-fail behavior.

d.text: `autoComplete = false` tells Spring Zeebe not to auto-complete the job after the handler returns. The handler takes responsibility for completion. scenario: A backend service must publish a Message to correlate with a waiting process instance (e.g., to release an Intermediate Message Catch Event). The correlation key is `orderId = "ORD-9876"`.

scenario: A team wants to fetch all completed process instances of a particular process definition for an export job. They have OAuth2 credentials and want to use a single REST endpoint with pagination.
