d.text: IDP's job is extraction, not orchestration. Routing decisions belong in BPMN. (Some IDP integrations may bundle simple rules, but the canonical separation is: IDP extracts, BPMN routes.) — IDP routes; BPMN layer.

scenario: A custom Connector's Element Template has a property `Slack Channel`. Modellers must enter a value starting with `#` (e.g., `#general`, `#alerts`). The team wants the template to reject non-conforming input at design time — before deployment.
question: Which Element Template constraint enforces this format?

options.text: Element Templates don't support regex constraints — incorrect; pattern + min/max-length are standard.
Invented constraint `constraints.startsWith: "#"` — not a real Element Template attribute.

Add a regex pattern constraint in the property's JSON: `"constraints": { "pattern": { "value": "^#[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$", "message": "Channel must start with # followed by alphanumerics, _, or -" } }`. Web Modeler validates at design time, blocking save or flagging an error.
Use a FEEL precondition — FEEL evaluates at runtime, not design time; the goal is design-time validation.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Element Template constraints include `pattern` (regex), `notEmpty`, `minLength`, `maxLength`. The `pattern` constraint with a meaningful `message` provides immediate design-time feedback in Web Modeler / Desktop Modeler — the modeller sees the error before deploying. This catches typos and convention violations early.

(b) Invented constraint: No such field. Modellers sometimes invent names hoping they match; for exact constraint names check the docs.

(c) Incorrect: Regex pattern is part of the Element Template constraints schema.

(d) Wrong layer: FEEL runs at process runtime — when the Service Task activates and the Connector evaluates its config. By then the BPMN is already deployed. To catch issues at design time, use Element Template constraints.

a.text: Regex pattern is part of the Element Template constraints schema. — ; pattern standard.

b.text: No such field. Modellers sometimes invent names hoping they match; for exact constraint names check the docs. — invented; real attribute.

c.text: Element Template constraints include `pattern` (regex), `notEmpty`, `minLength`, `maxLength`. The `pattern` constraint with a meaningful `message` provides immediate design-time feedback in Web Modeler / Desktop Modeler — the modeller sees the error before deploying. This catches typos and convention violations early. — constraints.pattern regex + message design-time validation.

d.text: FEEL runs at process runtime — when the Service Task activates and the Connector evaluates its config. By then the BPMN is already deployed. To catch issues at design time, use Element Template constraints. — wrong layer; FEEL runtime.

scenario: An AI Agent Connector orchestrates a customer-service agent. The LLM decides which tool to call next based on conversation context. The team's agent has 4 tools: `lookup-order`, `check-shipping-status`, `escalate-to-human`, `send-survey`. They're configuring where these tools are defined.
question: Where are the tools (the agent's callable actions) defined in the BPMN?

options.text: As JSON config in the AI Agent Connector's property panel — partial truth; the Connector might have some metadata properties, but the activities themselves are the source of truth for what tools exist. Tool registration is BPMN-driven, not JSON-config-driven.
Tools aren't configurable; the AI Agent has a hardcoded list — incorrect; the entire value proposition is BPMN-defined orchestration.

Inside the LLM provider's tool registry on the provider side (e.g., OpenAI Functions, Anthropic Tool Use) — that's how tools are passed to the LLM at inference time, but the source-of-truth definition is in BPMN, not on the LLM provider. Camunda passes BPMN-defined tools as functions in the LLM call.

As inner Service Tasks within an Ad-hoc Subprocess linked from the AI Agent Connector. Each Service Task = one callable tool. The AI Agent reads the available activities, builds a tool registry for the LLM, and at each turn lets the LLM choose which to invoke. The Service Task's task type, input mapping, and description (often visible to the LLM as the tool's docstring) describe what each tool does.

explanation:
(a) Correct: The canonical Camunda 8 agentic orchestration pattern: AI Agent Connector + Ad-hoc Subprocess. The Ad-hoc Subprocess hosts the available tools as inner activities (typically Service Tasks). The AI Agent at runtime reads the activity list (and their descriptions / schemas) and presents them to the LLM. Per turn: LLM picks one, Camunda executes it, result returns to LLM, repeat until done. The activities are the source of truth — change the tool by editing the BPMN.

(b) Partially: Connector properties may carry agent-level config (model, system prompt, etc.) but tool definitions live in the Ad-hoc Subprocess.

(c) Misleading direction: Tools are passed from Camunda to the LLM at call time as part of the request (e.g., function definitions for OpenAI). Source of truth is Camunda-side BPMN; LLM-side is just the transient request payload.

(d) Incorrect: Tools are fully configurable via BPMN.

a.text: Connector properties may carry agent-level config (model, system prompt, etc.) but tool definitions live in the Ad-hoc Subprocess. — partial — Connector agent-level config, tools inner activities.

b.text: Tools are fully configurable via BPMN. — ; fully configurable.

c.text: Tools are passed from Camunda to the LLM at call time as part of the request (e.g., function definitions for OpenAI). Source of truth is Camunda-side BPMN; LLM-side is just the transient request payload. — confuses serialization direction; source-of-truth BPMN.

d.text: The canonical Camunda 8 agentic orchestration pattern: AI Agent Connector + Ad-hoc Subprocess. The Ad-hoc Subprocess hosts the available tools as inner activities (typically Service Tasks). The AI Agent at runtime reads the activity list (and their descriptions / schemas) and presents them to the LLM. Per turn: LLM picks one, Camunda executes it, result returns to LLM, repeat until done. The activities are the source of truth — change the tool by editing the BPMN. — Tools = Ad-hoc inner Service Tasks; LLM sees activity list as tool registry.

scenario: A process must wait until tomorrow at 8:00 AM local time before continuing. The current time isn't known in advance — the timer must compute the moment dynamically based on `now()`.
question: Which Timer Date FEEL expression fits a "next day 8 AM" relative computation?

options.text: A natural-language expression `"tomorrow at 8 AM"` — not parseable by FEEL or BPMN.

A FEEL expression combining `today()` (returns today's date), adding `duration("P1D")` (one day), and combining with `time("08:00:00")` to form a `date and time` value. Example: `=date and time(today() + duration("P1D"), time("08:00:00"))` (exact form varies by FEEL implementation). The Timer Date type accepts FEEL date-and-time expressions, so this is dynamic per instance. Documentation: [Timer Events](https://docs.camunda.io/docs/components/modeler/bpmn/timer-events/) + [FEEL temporal](https://docs.camunda.io/docs/components/modeler/feel/builtin-functions/feel-built-in-functions-temporal/)
Cannot compute relative to `now()` — incorrect; FEEL temporal arithmetic handles this.
A static ISO 8601 datetime like `2026-05-15T08:00:00` — doesn't generalise; works for the next firing only, then becomes stale.

explanation:
(a) Correct: FEEL provides date constructors (`date()`, `time()`, `date and time()`, `today()`, `now()`) and duration arithmetic (`duration("P1D")`). Compose them to express any relative moment. The Timer Date attribute accepts a FEEL expression; at activation, the expression evaluates and the timer is scheduled for the resulting absolute moment. This pattern works for any "X days from now at time T" requirement.

(b) Static, brittle: A literal ISO datetime fires once at that specific moment. For an instance that activates later, it might be in the past — timer fires immediately. Not "relative tomorrow."

(c) Not parseable: Natural language isn't a FEEL syntax.

(d) Incorrect: FEEL temporal arithmetic is the canonical solution.

a.text: Natural language isn't a FEEL syntax.

b.text: FEEL provides date constructors (`date()`, `time()`, `date and time()`, `today()`, `now()`) and duration arithmetic (`duration("P1D")`). Compose them to express any relative moment. The Timer Date attribute accepts a FEEL expression; at activation, the expression evaluates and the timer is scheduled for the resulting absolute moment. This pattern works for any "X days from now at time T" requirement. — FEEL temporal composition computes relative moments dynamically.

c.text: FEEL temporal arithmetic is the canonical solution.

d.text: A literal ISO datetime fires once at that specific moment. For an instance that activates later, it might be in the past — timer fires immediately. Not "relative tomorrow." — static; not relative.

scenario: A Receive Task waits for a message named `OrderShipped` with correlation key FEEL expression `=orderId`. When the task activates, the process variable `orderId` happens to be `null` (it wasn't set upstream due to a data issue).
question: What happens at the moment of Receive Task activation with a null correlation key?

options.text: The correlation key defaults to an empty string — no such default; null is an error case.

The activation fails / creates an Incident — Zeebe requires the correlation key value to be non-null (and typically non-empty for strings) at the moment the subscription is created. Ops must fix the data (set `orderId`) and resolve the incident; the subscription is then created and the task waits for matching messages.
A random correlation key is generated — definitely not; random keys would mismatch any sane publish-message correlation.
The task waits forever silently — incorrect; Zeebe doesn't accept null correlation keys silently. The runtime surfaces an Incident so ops can act.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Zeebe's contract for Message correlation: at the moment a subscription is created (Receive Task activated or Message Catch Event reached), the correlation key value must be defined and non-null. If the FEEL expression evaluates to null, Zeebe creates an Incident with a clear error message ("correlation key is null" or similar). Ops sees this in Operate, updates the variable, resolves the incident, and the subscription is then created. This guard prevents silent miscorrelation issues.

(b) Wrong default behaviour: Silent waiting would be the worst outcome — the team wouldn't know anything's wrong until a published message inexplicably fails to correlate. Operate's incident-on-null is the right design.

(c) No empty-string default: Empty string would correlate to "" message keys, which is also dangerous. Null is an error case, not a default case.

(d) Not random: Random correlation would mismatch published messages; defeats the purpose.

a.text: Empty string would correlate to "" message keys, which is also dangerous. Null is an error case, not a default case. — no empty-string default.

b.text: Zeebe's contract for Message correlation: at the moment a subscription is created (Receive Task activated or Message Catch Event reached), the correlation key value must be defined and non-null. If the FEEL expression evaluates to null, Zeebe creates an Incident with a clear error message ("correlation key is null" or similar). Ops sees this in Operate, updates the variable, resolves the incident, and the subscription is then created. This guard prevents silent miscorrelation issues. — Null correlation key → Incident; ops fixes data and resolves.

c.text: Random correlation would mismatch published messages; defeats the purpose. — random correlation dangerous.

d.text: Silent waiting would be the worst outcome — the team wouldn't know anything's wrong until a published message inexplicably fails to correlate. Operate's incident-on-null is the right design. — silent wait worst outcome.

scenario: A Camunda 7 veteran joining a Camunda 8 team asks: "I used Task Listeners (on-create, on-assignment, on-complete) extensively in C7 for cross-cutting concerns like audit logging, dynamic assignment, validation. How do I model the equivalent in Camunda 8?"
question: What is Camunda 8's approach to Task-Listener-style cross-cutting concerns?

options.text: Use the `zeebe:taskListener` extension element — invented; not a Zeebe attribute.
Camunda 8 has a native Listener pattern equivalent to C7 — incorrect; the C7 listener mental model is not part of C8's design.
Set a `listenTo` attribute on Service Tasks — invented; doesn't exist.

Camunda 8 doesn't have Task Listeners like C7. The recommended approach is explicit BPMN modelling — express the cross-cutting concern as a visible BPMN element (e.g., a Service Task before/after for audit logging, an Event Subprocess with Message Start for dynamic concerns, FEEL expressions in assignmentDefinition for dynamic assignment). The philosophy: visible behaviour over hidden listeners.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Camunda 8's design philosophy intentionally moves away from hidden listeners. Cross-cutting concerns become explicit BPMN elements: audit logging = a Service Task before/after; dynamic assignment = FEEL expression in assignmentDefinition; validation = Service Task with worker that performs validation. The trade-off: BPMN diagrams have more visible elements, but the behaviour is transparent — no hidden execution sneaking in via listeners. This makes processes more readable and reasoning easier; C7 listeners were often invisible-but-impactful.

(b) Invented extension: `zeebe:taskListener` isn't part of the Zeebe BPMN extension schema.

(c) Invented attribute: No `listenTo` attribute.

(d) Inaccurate: No 1:1 listener equivalent; the migration path requires re-thinking listener uses as explicit BPMN.

a.text: `zeebe:taskListener` isn't part of the Zeebe BPMN extension schema. — invented extension.

b.text: No 1:1 listener equivalent; the migration path requires re-thinking listener uses as explicit BPMN. — wrong; no native equivalent.

c.text: No `listenTo` attribute. — invented attribute.

d.text: Camunda 8's design philosophy intentionally moves away from hidden listeners. Cross-cutting concerns become explicit BPMN elements: audit logging = a Service Task before/after; dynamic assignment = FEEL expression in assignmentDefinition; validation = Service Task with worker that performs validation. The trade-off: BPMN diagrams have more visible elements, but the behaviour is transparent — no hidden execution sneaking in via listeners. This makes processes more readable and reasoning easier; C7 listeners were often invisible-but-impactful. — C8 prefers explicit BPMN over hidden listeners.

scenario: A Service Task has Task Headers configured: `{priority: "high", retryHint: "5min"}`. These values are static — the same for every instance of this task. A new team member asks: "Can the headers be dynamic per instance — e.g., `priority` varying by customer tier?"
question: Can Task Headers be dynamic (vary per process instance)?

options.text: No — Task Headers are static metadata defined at design time (per modelled task, identical for every instance). They're not evaluated as FEEL expressions per instance. For dynamic per-instance values, use Input Mapping with FEEL expressions (which evaluate at activation and pass to the worker as local-scoped variables).
Yes — Headers support a mix of static and dynamic values via syntax markers. Incorrect; no such mixed-mode syntax.
Yes — Zeebe re-reads Header values each activation. Misleading; the broker reads the BPMN-modelled Headers, which are static.
Yes — Headers can contain FEEL expressions that evaluate per instance. Incorrect; Headers carry literal string values, not FEEL.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Task Headers are part of the BPMN model — string key/value pairs attached to a specific task element. Modelled once at design time; identical across instances. The worker reads them from `activatedJob.getCustomHeaders()`. For dynamic per-instance metadata, use Input Mapping: define a local variable via FEEL that evaluates against process variables / context, and pass it to the worker as a local-scoped variable. This separates "static config" (Headers) from "dynamic data" (variables).

(b) Incorrect: Headers are not FEEL-evaluated. They're literal string values.

(c) Misleading wording: The broker does read Headers each time the job activates, but the values themselves don't change between activations — they're static in the BPMN.

(d) Invented feature: No mixed-mode syntax.

a.text: Task Headers are part of the BPMN model — string key/value pairs attached to a specific task element. Modelled once at design time; identical across instances. The worker reads them from `activatedJob.getCustomHeaders()`. For dynamic per-instance metadata, use Input Mapping: define a local variable via FEEL that evaluates against process variables / context, and pass it to the worker as a local-scoped variable. This separates "static config" (Headers) from "dynamic data" (variables). — Headers static; use Input Mapping dynamic per-instance values.

b.text: No mixed-mode syntax. — invented mixed-mode syntax.

c.text: The broker does read Headers each time the job activates, but the values themselves don't change between activations — they're static in the BPMN. — misleading wording; Headers static.

d.text: Headers are not FEEL-evaluated. They're literal string values. — Headers FEEL-evaluated.

scenario: A risk-assessment DMN evaluates a customer against various risk indicators (history, geography, transaction patterns). Multiple rules can match a single customer. The team wants the decision to return a single number — the count of matching rules — representing how many risk indicators triggered. The downstream BPMN uses this count to route (0-1 indicators = low risk, 2+ = high risk).
question: Which hit policy + aggregator returns the count of matching rules?

options.text: UNIQUE — requires exactly one match; multiple matches raise an error. Wrong intent (we want multiple).
COLLECT with SUM aggregator over a constant `1` output — workable workaround: each matching rule outputs `1`, SUM gives the count. Achieves same result but COUNT is the direct expression.
RULE ORDER — returns the matches as a list ordered by rule order. The caller would then have to compute `count(matches)` separately; less direct.
COLLECT with COUNT aggregator — engine evaluates all rules, counts how many matched, returns the count as a single number. Designed exactly for cardinality reporting.

explanation:
(a) Correct: COLLECT supports four aggregators: SUM, MIN, MAX, COUNT. COUNT specifically counts how many rules matched, returning a single number. Perfect for cardinality questions like "how many risk indicators?" or "how many discounts apply?" The downstream consumer gets one number, no list parsing.

(b) Workable workaround: SUM over constant-1 outputs reproduces COUNT semantically. Sometimes useful when an output column already represents the contribution; e.g., "how many points does this rule add?" with variable point values would use SUM directly. For pure cardinality, COUNT is more declarative.

(c) Wrong policy: UNIQUE forbids multiple matches — exactly what we want to allow.

(d) Indirect: RULE ORDER returns a list; counting it requires downstream FEEL `count(list)`. COLLECT-COUNT does it inside the DMN.

a.text: UNIQUE forbids multiple matches — exactly what we want to allow. — UNIQUE multi-match; wrong intent.

b.text: SUM over constant-1 outputs reproduces COUNT semantically. Sometimes useful when an output column already represents the contribution; e.g., "how many points does this rule add?" with variable point values would use SUM directly. For pure cardinality, COUNT is more declarative. — workaround; SUM(1) achieves same COUNT direct.

c.text: RULE ORDER returns a list; counting it requires downstream FEEL `count(list)`. COLLECT-COUNT does it inside the DMN. — RULE ORDER returns list; downstream count() needed.

d.text: COLLECT supports four aggregators: SUM, MIN, MAX, COUNT. COUNT specifically counts how many rules matched, returning a single number. Perfect for cardinality questions like "how many risk indicators?" or "how many discounts apply?" The downstream consumer gets one number, no list parsing. — COLLECT-COUNT canonical cardinality.

scenario: A customer-tagging DMN evaluates a customer and may apply multiple tags like `"VIP"`, `"LOYAL"`, `"AT_RISK"`. The output is intentionally a list of strings — the BPMN downstream uses this list as the customer's tags collection.
question: Can a DMN decision return a list as its output, and how does the caller receive it?

options.text: Yes — DMN supports list outputs natively. The most common way: COLLECT hit policy (no aggregator) returns a list of all matching rules' outputs as a list. The caller (Business Rule Task) receives this list as the `resultVariable`. Downstream FEEL: `=tags contains "VIP"`.
No, DMN outputs are scalars only — incorrect; DMN spec supports lists via COLLECT and via list-typed outputs.
Yes, but the list requires manual decoding from a JSON string — incorrect; the engine returns structured list values, not JSON strings.
Yes, but only if all matching rules produce identical values — that's the ANY policy semantics (multi-match same output → single value). For lists you want COLLECT.

explanation:
(a) Correct: DMN decisions can return lists in two main ways: (1) COLLECT hit policy collects all matching rules' outputs into a list (no aggregator means "give me the list"); (2) a single output column typed as `list` returning a FEEL list expression. The Business Rule Task's `resultVariable` captures whatever the decision returns — list, scalar, or context — preserving structure. The caller's FEEL has full access: `tags contains "VIP"`, `count(tags) > 0`, `tags[1]`, etc.

(b) Wrong: DMN supports lists.

(c) Wrong policy: ANY allows multiple matches but constrains them to produce identical outputs; result is the single value (not a list). For a list of varied tags, COLLECT is the right policy.

(d) Wrong serialization: The engine returns native list values, FEEL-navigable. No JSON-string decoding step.

a.text: DMN decisions can return lists in two main ways: (1) COLLECT hit policy collects all matching rules' outputs into a list (no aggregator means "give me the list"); (2) a single output column typed as `list` returning a FEEL list expression. The Business Rule Task's `resultVariable` captures whatever the decision returns — list, scalar, or context — preserving structure. The caller's FEEL has full access: `tags contains "VIP"`, `count(tags) > 0`, `tags[1]`, etc. — COLLECT returns list natively.

b.text: DMN supports lists.

c.text: The engine returns native list values, FEEL-navigable. No JSON-string decoding step. — no JSON serialization.

d.text: ANY allows multiple matches but constrains them to produce identical outputs; result is the single value (not a list). For a list of varied tags, COLLECT is the right policy. — confuses ANY with COLLECT.

scenario: A BPMN Business Rule Task invokes a DMN decision. The DMN's table has output columns named `category` (string) and `score` (number). The task has `resultVariable = "decision"`. The team is wondering how to access the two output fields downstream.
question: How are DMN output columns exposed to downstream BPMN via resultVariable?

options.text: The `resultVariable` (here `decision`) holds the entire result object. DMN output column names become field names of that object. Downstream FEEL: `=decision.category`, `=decision.score`. For decisions with a single output column, some engines flatten to the bare value; check your Zeebe / DMN runtime semantics.

Output column names become process variables directly (so `=category` and `=score` work without resultVariable) — partial truth in some engines, but Camunda 8's canonical contract uses resultVariable as the named result holder.
Always called `result` regardless of configuration — incorrect; resultVariable specifies the name.
DMN auto-creates a variable named after the decision ID — invented; resultVariable is the canonical naming knob.

explanation:
(a) Correct: The contract: `resultVariable` names the top-level holder; DMN output columns become its fields. So with `resultVariable = "decision"` and outputs `category` + `score`, you get `decision.category` and `decision.score` in process scope. The structure preserves the DMN model. Note: when the DMN has a single output column (no name needed), some engines flatten the result to the bare value; multi-column always gives a structured object. Always verify with the Zeebe version's docs.

(b) Partial misconception: Some DMN runtimes allow column names to become top-level variables directly. Camunda 8's contract makes resultVariable the explicit holder; column names appear as its fields.

(c) Invented: No auto-naming-from-decision-ID.

(d) Incorrect: resultVariable can be any name.

a.text: The contract: `resultVariable` names the top-level holder; DMN output columns become its fields. So with `resultVariable = "decision"` and outputs `category` + `score`, you get `decision.category` and `decision.score` in process scope. The structure preserves the DMN model. Note: when the DMN has a single output column (no name needed), some engines flatten the result to the bare value; multi-column always gives a structured object. Always verify with the Zeebe version's docs. — resultVariable the holder; output columns fields.

b.text: Some DMN runtimes allow column names to become top-level variables directly. Camunda 8's contract makes resultVariable the explicit holder; column names appear as its fields. — partial — depends on engine flavour; canonical C8.

c.text: resultVariable can be any name. — incorrect; resultVariable custom.

d.text: No auto-naming-from-decision-ID. — invented auto-naming.

scenario: A commission-calculation DMN table has an output column `commission`. The team wants a rule to compute commission as a FEEL expression based on inputs — e.g., `=input.amount * 0.1`. They're confirming that FEEL expressions are allowed in output entries.
question: Are FEEL expressions allowed in DMN output entries (the cells in the output column)?

options.text: No, output entries are literals only — incorrect; FEEL expressions are core to DMN.

Yes, but the expression must be prefixed with `=` (or similar marker) — depending on tooling, some modellers require the prefix; others treat output cells as FEEL by default. Check your Web Modeler's convention.

Yes — DMN output entries are FEEL expressions. They can reference any input value, call FEEL built-in functions (like `decimal()`, `if-then-else`, list functions), and reference variables in context. The cell's value at evaluation time is the result of the FEEL expression. Documentation: [DMN](https://docs.camunda.io/docs/components/modeler/dmn/decision-table/) + [FEEL](https://docs.camunda.io/docs/components/modeler/feel/)
Yes, but only for numeric outputs (strings must be literals) — partial misconception; FEEL works for all types.

explanation:
(a) Correct: DMN's value proposition is declarative decision logic using FEEL. Output entries are FEEL expressions that evaluate against the rule's matching inputs (and any other context variables). Common uses: arithmetic from inputs (`=input.amount * 0.1`), conditional outputs (`=if input.tier = "GOLD" then "PREMIUM" else "STANDARD"`), list constructions, date arithmetic. Full FEEL is available.

(b) Wrong: Literal-only outputs would be very limiting; DMN explicitly supports expressions.

(c) Wrong type restriction: No type-based limitation; FEEL works for numerics, strings, dates, booleans, lists, contexts.

(d) Tooling-specific quirk: Web Modeler's DMN editor sometimes treats output entries as FEEL by default (no `=` needed), sometimes requires the prefix. The functional answer is "yes, FEEL expressions are allowed"; the prefix convention is a syntactic detail.

a.text: Literal-only outputs would be very limiting; DMN explicitly supports expressions. — wrong; output FEEL core.

b.text: Web Modeler's DMN editor sometimes treats output entries as FEEL by default (no `=` needed), sometimes requires the prefix. The functional answer is "yes, FEEL expressions are allowed"; the prefix convention is a syntactic detail. — partial — tooling-specific prefix detail.

c.text: DMN's value proposition is declarative decision logic using FEEL. Output entries are FEEL expressions that evaluate against the rule's matching inputs (and any other context variables). Common uses: arithmetic from inputs (`=input.amount * 0.1`), conditional outputs (`=if input.tier = "GOLD" then "PREMIUM" else "STANDARD"`), list constructions, date arithmetic. Full FEEL is available. — FEEL expressions allowed; full FEEL power in output entries.

d.text: No type-based limitation; FEEL works for numerics, strings, dates, booleans, lists, contexts. — wrong type restriction.

scenario: A complex DMN model has Decision A and Decision B. Decision A's body references Decision B's result — e.g., A computes a final score by combining B's risk rating with other inputs. The DRD shows an Information Requirement arrow from B (supplier) to A (consumer).
question: How does Decision A invoke Decision B in its FEEL body?

options.text: Reference Decision B's name in a FEEL expression in A's body — the engine resolves the reference using the DRD dependency. When A evaluates, the engine first evaluates B (per the Information Requirement arrow), makes B's result available in A's evaluation context, then evaluates A. Example: A's expression might be `=B + otherFactor`.
Explicit `invoke(B)` call — invented; FEEL doesn't have such a function. The DRD dependency + name reference is the mechanism.
Auto-resolved without explicit reference — incorrect; A must reference B by name to use its result. The DRD arrow declares the dependency; the FEEL reference uses it.

Manual coordination at BPMN level — invoke B's Business Rule Task first, then A's — incorrect for DMN-internal chaining; the BPMN should invoke only the top-level decision, and the DRD handles the chain.

explanation:
(a) Correct: DMN's DRD specifies the dependency graph; FEEL references use names declared in the DRD. The engine evaluates dependencies in topological order: when A's evaluation begins, B has already been evaluated (because of the arrow), and B's result is in A's context. A's FEEL body can reference `B` like any other variable.

(b) Invented function: No `invoke()` FEEL function; the DRD + name reference is the mechanism.

(c) Misconception: The DRD doesn't auto-substitute; A still must reference B's name in its body. The DRD only declares "A may use B"; FEEL provides the use.

(d) Wrong scope: Multiple Business Rule Tasks for chained decisions is the BPMN-level orchestration anti-pattern when DMN can handle the chain internally. BPMN should invoke the top-level decision; DMN handles upstream dependencies automatically.

a.text: DMN's DRD specifies the dependency graph; FEEL references use names declared in the DRD. The engine evaluates dependencies in topological order: when A's evaluation begins, B has already been evaluated (because of the arrow), and B's result is in A's context. A's FEEL body can reference `B` like any other variable. — FEEL reference + DRD dependency.

b.text: No `invoke()` FEEL function; the DRD + name reference is the mechanism. — invented function.

c.text: The DRD doesn't auto-substitute; A still must reference B's name in its body. The DRD only declares "A may use B"; FEEL provides the use. — misconception about auto-substitution.

d.text: Multiple Business Rule Tasks for chained decisions is the BPMN-level orchestration anti-pattern when DMN can handle the chain internally. BPMN should invoke the top-level decision; DMN handles upstream dependencies automatically. — wrong scope; BPMN over-decomposes.

scenario: A team's Business Rule Task evaluates a DMN. The team is curious about the runtime architecture — does Zeebe spawn a separate process / call out to a DMN engine, or evaluate inline?
question: How does Zeebe evaluate DMN decisions invoked from Business Rule Tasks?

options.text: Native engine — Zeebe has a built-in DMN evaluator (the FEEL engine + DMN runtime). Business Rule Tasks evaluate the referenced decision inline within the broker (or via the deployed component handling decision evaluation). No external worker subscription, no separate DMN engine process.
Via an external DMN service — incorrect; native.
On a different broker partition dedicated to DMN — incorrect; DMN evaluation is part of normal broker work.
Via a Job Worker subscribed to a special DMN task type — C7-style pattern; not how C8 handles DMN.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Camunda 8's runtime architecture includes native DMN evaluation. The FEEL engine and DMN runtime are bundled; Business Rule Tasks evaluate decisions inline without needing a separate worker subscription. This means: deploy the DMN, reference it from a Business Rule Task, instances evaluate it directly. Less moving parts, lower latency than C7's external-engine pattern.

(b) C7 reflex: In C7, DMN was sometimes handled by external workers. C8 builds it in; no need for a `dmn-eval` worker.

(c) Wrong architecture: No external DMN service.

(d) Wrong partition concept: DMN evaluation is just regular broker work; no dedicated partition.

a.text: Camunda 8's runtime architecture includes native DMN evaluation. The FEEL engine and DMN runtime are bundled; Business Rule Tasks evaluate decisions inline without needing a separate worker subscription. This means: deploy the DMN, reference it from a Business Rule Task, instances evaluate it directly. Less moving parts, lower latency than C7's external-engine pattern. — Native DMN evaluation Zeebe.

b.text: No external DMN service. — wrong architecture.

c.text: DMN evaluation is just regular broker work; no dedicated partition. — wrong partition concept.

d.text: In C7, DMN was sometimes handled by external workers. C8 builds it in; no need for a `dmn-eval` worker. — C7 reflex.

scenario: A DMN decision-table input column `applicationDate` is typed as `date`. A rule's input entry is `< date("2025-01-01")`. The input value at evaluation is `date("2024-12-15")`.
question: Does the rule match?

options.text: No, dates must be exactly equal — incorrect; FEEL supports `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` on dates.

Depends on time zone — partial truth; pure `date` values don't carry time zones (they're date-only). For `date and time` values, time zone matters. Here both are `date` only, so no time-zone ambiguity.

Yes — FEEL's date comparison is temporal (chronological): `date("2024-12-15") < date("2025-01-01")` is `true`. The unary test `< date("2025-01-01")` matches any date earlier than 2025-01-01, which includes 2024-12-15.
Type mismatch error — strings can't compare to dates — incorrect; both sides are `date()` values (the constructor parses ISO strings to date type).

explanation:
(a) Correct: FEEL implements temporal arithmetic for dates: `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `=`, `!=` all work and respect chronological order. `2024-12-15 < 2025-01-01` is `true`. The DMN rule with input entry `< date("2025-01-01")` matches.

(b) Wrong: FEEL supports comparison operators, not just equality.

(c) Wrong type analysis: Both `date("2024-12-15")` and `date("2025-01-01")` are date values (constructor converts strings). Comparison is between dates, not string-vs-date.

(d) Wrong type concern: Pure `date` type is date-only (no time, no zone). Time zone matters for `date and time` type. Question uses `date()` constructor; no time component.

a.text: FEEL supports comparison operators, not just equality. — exact-equal-only is wrong.

b.text: Pure `date` type is date-only (no time, no zone). Time zone matters for `date and time` type. Question uses `date()` constructor; no time component. — partial — time zone matters for date and time, date only.

c.text: FEEL implements temporal arithmetic for dates: `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `=`, `!=` all work and respect chronological order. `2024-12-15 < 2025-01-01` is `true`. The DMN rule with input entry `< date("2025-01-01")` matches. — Temporal comparison; 2024-12-15 < 2025-01-01.

d.text: Both `date("2024-12-15")` and `date("2025-01-01")` are date values (constructor converts strings). Comparison is between dates, not string-vs-date. — wrong type analysis; both date values.

scenario: A registration form has an "Email" field that must be required — users can't submit the form without filling it. The team also wants the form's UI to visually indicate which fields are mandatory (typically a red asterisk next to the label).
question: Which Form field property handles required validation AND triggers the visual indicator?

options.text: Configure CSS in a custom theme to highlight required fields — wrong layer; CSS doesn't validate.

Set `validate.required: true` on the field. The Form renderer applies validation at submit time (rejecting empty inputs) and automatically adds the visual required indicator (asterisk) next to the field label. One property handles both UX requirements.
Manually add `*` to the label text — workable cosmetic shortcut but doesn't actually validate; users can still submit empty. Splits concerns: cosmetic indicator separate from real validation.
Required validation isn't supported in Camunda Forms — incorrect; required is a basic validation feature.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Camunda Forms' validation property `required` does double duty: (1) at submit time, the form rejects empty values and shows an error message; (2) at render time, the field gets the required indicator (typically an asterisk) next to the label. This is the canonical, single-source-of-truth way to express mandatory fields. The indicator is automatic — no manual styling needed.

(b) Cosmetic but unsafe: Adding `*` to the label visually communicates "required" but doesn't actually enforce. Users see the indicator, submit empty, form accepts — defeats the contract. Always pair visual cues with real validation.

(c) Wrong layer: CSS handles visual styling, not validation. Even if CSS detected empty fields (via `:invalid`), it can't reject submission.

(d) Incorrect: `validate.required` is a foundational property.

a.text: CSS handles visual styling, not validation. Even if CSS detected empty fields (via `:invalid`), it can't reject submission. — wrong layer; CSS validates.

b.text: Camunda Forms' validation property `required` does double duty: (1) at submit time, the form rejects empty values and shows an error message; (2) at render time, the field gets the required indicator (typically an asterisk) next to the label. This is the canonical, single-source-of-truth way to express mandatory fields. The indicator is automatic — no manual styling needed. — validate.required handles both validation visual indicator.

c.text: Adding `*` to the label visually communicates "required" but doesn't actually enforce. Users see the indicator, submit empty, form accepts — defeats the contract. Always pair visual cues with real validation. — cosmetic only; enforces.

d.text: `validate.required` is a foundational property. — ; required is supported.

scenario: A medical-intake form has a Numeric field "Number of dependents." The team needs the field to be mandatory (user must fill it), but `0` is a valid value (a single person has 0 dependents). They're nervous that "required" might confuse with "non-zero."
question: Which validation expresses "user must enter a value, including 0"?

options.text: `validate.required: true` — the required check is "field has a value present" (not blank, not null). `0` counts as a valid present value; it's a number, not "absence." So `required: true` correctly allows 0 while requiring an entry.
Mark the field as `notNullable` in schema — invented attribute; not part of Camunda Forms validation vocabulary.

Regex pattern `^[0-9]+$` — partial: works if you treat the field as a string, but blank input matches the empty regex `^$`? No — `^[0-9]+$` requires at least one digit, so empty input would fail regex. But this conflates validation purposes; required is the right tool for presence.
`validate.min: 0` — covers "value ≥ 0" but doesn't catch blank input; user could submit nothing and `min` wouldn't trigger (because there's no value to compare).

explanation:
(a) Correct: Camunda Forms' `required: true` enforces presence — a value must be entered. For numeric fields, this means the user submitted a number (any number, including 0). The semantic gap between "required" (presence) and "non-zero" (numeric value) is well-defined: required = "did you fill it?"; min/max = "is the value in range?" They compose: `required: true, min: 0` means "fill it, and the number must be ≥ 0."

(b) Misses presence check: `min: 0` checks numeric bounds when a value is present. If the user leaves the field blank, `min` has nothing to compare and doesn't trigger. Use `required` for presence.

(c) Awkward for numerics: Regex on numeric inputs is generally a code smell; numeric validation has dedicated tools (`min`, `max`, `step`). The regex `^[0-9]+$` would technically work but is opaque.

(d) Invented: `notNullable` isn't a Camunda Forms attribute.

a.text: Camunda Forms' `required: true` enforces presence — a value must be entered. For numeric fields, this means the user submitted a number (any number, including 0). The semantic gap between "required" (presence) and "non-zero" (numeric value) is well-defined: required = "did you fill it?"; min/max = "is the value in range?" They compose: `required: true, min: 0` means "fill it, and the number must be ≥ 0." — required = presence check; 0 valid present value.

b.text: `notNullable` isn't a Camunda Forms attribute. — invented attribute.

c.text: Regex on numeric inputs is generally a code smell; numeric validation has dedicated tools (`min`, `max`, `step`). The regex `^[0-9]+$` would technically work but is opaque. — awkward for numerics; regex code smell.

d.text: `min: 0` checks numeric bounds when a value is present. If the user leaves the field blank, `min` has nothing to compare and doesn't trigger. Use `required` for presence. — partial; misses presence check (blank passes).

scenario: A complex onboarding form needs rich-text static content between fields — section headers in bold, bulleted lists of instructions, hyperlinks to policy docs. The team wants to use Markdown for the rich text.
question: Which Forms component supports Markdown rendering for static rich-text content?

options.text: No Markdown support; plain text only — incorrect; rich-text rendering is part of the Forms element library.
Embed an `<iframe>` pointing to an external Markdown renderer — overkill; Forms render Markdown natively.
Embed HTML directly — wrong abstraction; Forms abstracts above HTML to enable cross-renderer compatibility (Tasklist web UI, embedded React app, etc.).

The Text View component (or equivalent heading / paragraph components) typically supports Markdown rendering. Set the content property to a Markdown string; the form renders it as formatted HTML (bold, lists, links, etc.).

explanation:
(a) Correct: Camunda Forms' element library includes static-text components (Text View, Heading, etc.) that support Markdown. The modeller types Markdown in the content property; the renderer converts to HTML at display time. Standard Markdown features work: bold, *italic*, bullet lists, numbered lists, hyperlinks, headings, blockquotes. This is the canonical way to embed rich documentation/help text in a form.

(b) Wrong: Markdown support is documented in the Forms element library.

(c) Wrong layer: Forms abstract above HTML for portability across renderers. Raw HTML embedding isn't the form-spec way; Markdown is.

(d) Overkill: iframes for static text inside a form would be wasteful and break form integration.

a.text: Markdown support is documented in the Forms element library. — wrong; Markdown supported.

b.text: iframes for static text inside a form would be wasteful and break form integration. — overkill.

c.text: Forms abstract above HTML for portability across renderers. Raw HTML embedding isn't the form-spec way; Markdown is. — wrong layer; Forms abstract above HTML.

d.text: Camunda Forms' element library includes static-text components (Text View, Heading, etc.) that support Markdown. The modeller types Markdown in the content property; the renderer converts to HTML at display time. Standard Markdown features work: bold, *italic*, bullet lists, numbered lists, hyperlinks, headings, blockquotes. This is the canonical way to embed rich documentation/help text in a form. — Text View / Heading components support Markdown.

scenario: A retail process sends Slack notifications for high-value orders. The team adds a Slack Outbound Connector to a Service Task. They're configuring authentication — Slack offers several mechanisms.
question: Which authentication methods does the Slack Outbound Connector typically support?

options.text: Username + password — incorrect; Slack deprecated password-based API access years ago.
mTLS only — wrong protocol layer; Slack uses HTTPS with token-based auth, not mTLS for individual API calls.
Anonymous — incorrect; Slack APIs always require some authentication.

OAuth Bot/User tokens OR Incoming Webhook URLs — Slack apps can be installed in a workspace yielding an OAuth token (bot or user-scoped); alternatively, an Incoming Webhook URL targets a specific channel without an app install. The Slack Connector supports both modes; configure via secrets to avoid leaking credentials in BPMN.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Slack's modern auth model uses OAuth (Bot tokens for app-style integration, User tokens for user-context calls) or Incoming Webhook URLs (simpler, channel-specific). The Connector exposes both options. Bot tokens are best for rich integrations (post to multiple channels, read messages); Webhook URLs are simplest when you just need to post to one channel. Both go through cluster secrets.

(b) Deprecated: Slack removed password-based API access for security.

(c) Incorrect: Slack APIs require auth.

(d) Wrong protocol: mTLS isn't Slack's API auth model.

a.text: Slack removed password-based API access for security. — deprecated.

b.text: mTLS isn't Slack's API auth model. — wrong protocol.

c.text: Slack APIs require auth. — incorrect.

d.text: Slack's modern auth model uses OAuth (Bot tokens for app-style integration, User tokens for user-context calls) or Incoming Webhook URLs (simpler, channel-specific). The Connector exposes both options. Bot tokens are best for rich integrations (post to multiple channels, read messages); Webhook URLs are simplest when you just need to post to one channel. Both go through cluster secrets. — OAuth tokens / Incoming Webhook URLs.

scenario: An Outbound HTTP Connector calls a partner API. The team needs to debug an intermittent failure — they want to see the HTTP response body the partner returned, but don't want the response stored as a process variable (privacy + scope cleanliness).
question: Which approach lets the team see the Connector's response body for debugging without persisting it as a process variable?

options.text: Operate doesn't show Connector responses — incorrect; Operate has visibility into Connector execution.

Camunda auto-logs all HTTP responses to a central log — partial truth depending on configuration; logs may capture some details but accessing them requires log infrastructure. Operate's execution details is the integrated path.
Cannot view responses; HTTP Connector silently discards body — incorrect; the Connector keeps the response, and Operate / logs surface it.

Use Operate's execution details view — for Service Task instances with Connectors, Operate shows execution metadata including (where supported) Connector responses, request/response logs, and error messages. The data lives in Operate's history, not in the process variable scope. For ephemeral debug, this is the canonical observability path. Documentation: [Operate](https://docs.camunda.io/docs/components/operate/) + [Connectors](https://docs.camunda.io/docs/components/connectors/use-connectors/)

explanation:
(a) Correct: Operate's instance detail page provides observability into individual flow nodes, including Connector executions. For HTTP Connector calls, you can see the request, response body (where the integration captures it), error messages on failure, and timing. This is the integrated debugging path that doesn't pollute process variables. For deeper inspection, configure structured logging in the Connector runtime.

(b) Partial: Logs exist but accessing them requires log-aggregation tooling (ELK / Loki etc.). Operate is the integrated, in-product path.

(c) Incorrect: Operate does provide visibility.

(d) Incorrect: Connectors don't discard responses; they keep them for Output Mapping / debugging.

a.text: Operate does provide visibility. — wrong; Operate has visibility.

b.text: Logs exist but accessing them requires log-aggregation tooling (ELK / Loki etc.). Operate is the integrated, in-product path. — partial — log aggregation, integrated.

c.text: Connectors don't discard responses; they keep them for Output Mapping / debugging. — wrong; responses discarded.

d.text: Operate's instance detail page provides observability into individual flow nodes, including Connector executions. For HTTP Connector calls, you can see the request, response body (where the integration captures it), error messages on failure, and timing. This is the integrated debugging path that doesn't pollute process variables. For deeper inspection, configure structured logging in the Connector runtime. — Operate execution details + Connector logs.

scenario: A team's custom Outbound Connector must call a partner API requiring JWT authentication — the partner gave them a client ID and secret, and a token endpoint to exchange for short-lived JWT access tokens. The team is implementing the auth flow inside the Connector code.
question: Where is the JWT auth logic implemented?

options.text: Skip JWT entirely; rely on the cluster's auth — incorrect; cluster auth governs Camunda's own APIs, not the partner's.

Inside the Connector's `OutboundConnectorFunction.execute()` method (or equivalent SDK entry point) — implement the OAuth2 / JWT flow: POST to the token endpoint with client credentials, receive the JWT, attach `Authorization: Bearer <jwt>` to outbound API calls. Cache the token (e.g., in a static field with expiry) to avoid re-fetching on every call. The Connector SDK exposes secrets via context to read client credentials safely.
Pass JWT as a BPMN process variable from upstream — leaks credentials to process scope (visible in Operate, audit logs); insecure.

Camunda Connector SDK has built-in auto-JWT — partial truth; some specific Connectors handle their auth internally, but for a custom Connector with a partner-specific token flow, the implementation is in your code.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Custom Outbound Connectors are responsible for whatever auth their target API requires. Inside the `execute()` method (or equivalent), implement the OAuth2 / JWT flow: read client_id and client_secret from `context.getSecret(...)` (via cluster secrets, never hardcoded), POST to the token endpoint, parse the JWT response, attach as Bearer. Cache the token using its `expiresIn`; only re-fetch when expired. Most JWT libraries provide caching helpers.

(b) Misleading: No "auto-JWT" magic in the generic SDK. Specific OOB Connectors handle their auth; custom Connectors handle theirs.

(c) Wrong concept: Cluster auth doesn't apply to partner APIs.

(d) Insecure: Process variables are observable; credentials shouldn't appear there.

a.text: Cluster auth doesn't apply to partner APIs. — wrong concept.

b.text: Custom Outbound Connectors are responsible for whatever auth their target API requires. Inside the `execute()` method (or equivalent), implement the OAuth2 / JWT flow: read client_id and client_secret from `context.getSecret(...)` (via cluster secrets, never hardcoded), POST to the token endpoint, parse the JWT response, attach as Bearer. Cache the token using its `expiresIn`; only re-fetch when expired. Most JWT libraries provide caching helpers. — JWT flow inside Connector code, secrets via context.

c.text: Process variables are observable; credentials shouldn't appear there. — insecure; credentials leak.

d.text: No "auto-JWT" magic in the generic SDK. Specific OOB Connectors handle their auth; custom Connectors handle theirs. — misleading; no auto-JWT custom.

scenario: An Inbound Webhook Connector is configured to start a process when a partner POSTs to it. The team wants to test the webhook locally before the partner integrates — they want to confirm: (1) the webhook URL is reachable, (2) a POST starts a process instance, (3) the body is correctly mapped to variables.
question: Which approach lets the team test the webhook without partner involvement?

options.text: After deploying the BPMN, get the Webhook URL from Web Modeler / Operate (or compute from cluster URL + Connector ID). Send test POST requests with curl, Postman, or any HTTP client — same body shape the partner will send. Verify in Operate that a process instance starts and that variables hold the expected values. This is the standard integration-testing path.
Set up a mock partner service that produces realistic POSTs — overkill for testing; curl / Postman is faster.
Webhook auto-tests itself on deploy — incorrect; no auto-test. Manual test required.
Webhook can't be tested without the partner system being live — incorrect; the URL is exposed and accepts any HTTP POST matching its auth/schema rules.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Webhooks are HTTP endpoints — any HTTP client can POST to them. The standard workflow: deploy, copy the Webhook URL, craft a test POST with curl or Postman matching the expected body shape, observe in Operate that a new instance starts with the right variables. Iterate until correct. Once it works, give the URL to the partner. Quick, deterministic, no partner dependency.

(b) Wrong: The URL accepts any matching POST; partner involvement is needed only for the real production traffic, not for testing.

(c) Overkill: Mock partner is useful for testing complex multi-step interactions, not for simple webhook validation.

(d) Incorrect: No auto-test.

a.text: Webhooks are HTTP endpoints — any HTTP client can POST to them. The standard workflow: deploy, copy the Webhook URL, craft a test POST with curl or Postman matching the expected body shape, observe in Operate that a new instance starts with the right variables. Iterate until correct. Once it works, give the URL to the partner. Quick, deterministic, no partner dependency. — curl/Postman POST to deployed Webhook URL.

b.text: Mock partner is useful for testing complex multi-step interactions, not for simple webhook validation. — overkill simple webhook test.

c.text: No auto-test. — no auto-test.

d.text: The URL accepts any matching POST; partner involvement is needed only for the real production traffic, not for testing. — wrong; URL accepts any POST.

scenario: A FEEL expression must add a value to the end of a list: `tags = ["A", "B"]` → produce `["A", "B", "C"]`. The team is choosing between FEEL built-ins and operators.
question: Which FEEL built-in fits "append element to list"?

options.text: `tags.push("C")` — JavaScript-style method call; not part of FEEL syntax.
`tags + ["C"]` — looks like list concatenation, but `+` operator on lists isn't FEEL-spec; lists concatenate via `concatenate(...)`.
`append(tags, "C")` — FEEL's standard list-append built-in. Returns a new list with the value added at the end.
`concatenate(tags, ["C"])` — works because `concatenate` joins lists, but for adding a single element `append` is the direct, semantic choice.

explanation:
(a) Correct: FEEL's `append(list, item)` is the dedicated function for "list + single element." Returns a new list (FEEL lists are immutable; functions return new lists rather than mutating). Semantic and concise.

(b) Wrong syntax: Method-call dot notation isn't FEEL. FEEL uses function-call syntax: `function(args)`.

(c) Wrong operator: `+` is numeric addition / string concatenation in FEEL, not list concatenation. Using `+` on lists is a type error.

(d) Workable: `concatenate(list1, list2)` joins lists; wrapping the single item in a singleton list `["C"]` works, but `append(tags, "C")` is more direct for the single-item case.

a.text: Method-call dot notation isn't FEEL. FEEL uses function-call syntax: `function(args)`. — JS reflex; FEEL syntax.

b.text: `+` is numeric addition / string concatenation in FEEL, not list concatenation. Using `+` on lists is a type error. — `+` lists concatenation FEEL.

c.text: FEEL's `append(list, item)` is the dedicated function for "list + single element." Returns a new list (FEEL lists are immutable; functions return new lists rather than mutating). Semantic and concise. — append() the dedicated FEEL function.

d.text: `concatenate(list1, list2)` joins lists; wrapping the single item in a singleton list `["C"]` works, but `append(tags, "C")` is more direct for the single-item case. — workable; concatenate works append directer.

scenario: A FEEL expression must remove an element by position from a list: `tags = ["A", "B", "C"]` → remove position 2 → `["A", "C"]`. (Position 2 is "B" since FEEL is 1-indexed.)
question: Which FEEL built-in fits?

options.text: `remove(tags, 2)` — FEEL's dedicated function for "list minus element at position." Returns a new list excluding the element at the given 1-indexed position.
`tags[!2]` — invented syntax; FEEL doesn't have a negate-index operator.
`tags.splice(2, 1)` — JavaScript Array method; not part of FEEL.
`delete(tags, 2)` — invented function name; FEEL uses `remove`.

explanation:
(a) Correct: `remove(list, position)` is FEEL's element-removal-by-position function. 1-indexed. Returns a new list (immutable lists). Use this when you know the position; for removal by value, FEEL has different patterns (filter / `[item in list : item != target]`).

(b) JS reflex: Method-call syntax with `.splice()` isn't FEEL.

(c) Wrong name: Not a FEEL function. Sometimes JS / Python users guess names.

(d) Invented syntax: FEEL list indexing supports positive integers (1-based) and negative integers (counting from end), but no `!` negate-index.

a.text: `remove(list, position)` is FEEL's element-removal-by-position function. 1-indexed. Returns a new list (immutable lists). Use this when you know the position; for removal by value, FEEL has different patterns (filter / `[item in list : item != target]`). — remove(list, position) FEEL standard.

b.text: FEEL list indexing supports positive integers (1-based) and negative integers (counting from end), but no `!` negate-index. — invented syntax.

c.text: Method-call syntax with `.splice()` isn't FEEL. — JS reflex.

d.text: Not a FEEL function. Sometimes JS / Python users guess names. — wrong name.

scenario: A FEEL expression must return the current date and time for use as a timestamp — e.g., to log when something happened.
question: Which FEEL built-in fits?

options.text: `Date.now()` — JavaScript syntax; not FEEL.
`time()` — `time()` is a constructor that parses a time string (e.g., `time("08:30:00")`), not "current time." Used to build time values from literals.
`now()` — returns the current `date and time` value. The canonical FEEL function for "current moment."
`currentTime()` — invented function name; FEEL uses `now()` for date-time and `today()` for date only.

explanation:
(a) Correct: `now()` returns the current `date and time` value (zoned). Use it as a timestamp source. Companion: `today()` returns just the current date (no time component). Both are zero-argument; the engine resolves them at expression evaluation.

(b) Wrong name: Modellers familiar with other languages sometimes try `currentTime()`. FEEL's spec uses `now()`.

(c) Wrong purpose: `time(str)` is a constructor — parses a time string to a time value. Doesn't give "now."

(d) JS reflex: Not FEEL syntax.

a.text: Not FEEL syntax. — JS reflex.

b.text: `time(str)` is a constructor — parses a time string to a time value. Doesn't give "now." — wrong purpose (constructor, current).

c.text: `now()` returns the current `date and time` value (zoned). Use it as a timestamp source. Companion: `today()` returns just the current date (no time component). Both are zero-argument; the engine resolves them at expression evaluation. — now() canonical current moment.

d.text: Modellers familiar with other languages sometimes try `currentTime()`. FEEL's spec uses `now()`. — wrong name.

scenario: A Java application uses Spring Zeebe and hosts many workers handling many small jobs. The team observes underutilisation — the workers seem to wait for jobs instead of running them concurrently. They want intra-JVM parallelism — multiple threads handling jobs concurrently within one process.
question: Which Spring Zeebe configuration tunes intra-JVM concurrency?

options.text: Spring Zeebe is single-threaded per JVM; spawn multiple JVMs for parallelism — incorrect; Spring Zeebe supports multi-threaded execution within a JVM.
Not configurable; tuning is hands-off — incorrect; multiple Spring Zeebe properties tune concurrency.
Spawn multiple JVMs (Docker containers) to get parallelism — workable for horizontal scaling but doesn't tune intra-JVM; should also tune pool size.

Configure the executor thread pool size (Spring Zeebe property like `camunda.client.zeebe.execution-threads` or equivalent) and ensure `maxJobsActive` is high enough to keep threads fed. The pool's threads process activated jobs concurrently; maxJobsActive controls how many jobs the worker holds at once.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Intra-JVM parallelism in Spring Zeebe is controlled by two related knobs: (1) executor thread pool — how many threads in the JVM process activated jobs in parallel; (2) maxJobsActive — how many jobs the worker subscription activates at a time. For full utilisation, threads ≥ maxJobsActive (otherwise threads sit idle while jobs queue). Tune based on workload: CPU-bound work → match thread count to CPU cores; I/O-bound work → can over-subscribe (more threads than cores).

(b) Wrong: Spring Zeebe is multi-threaded; default pool sizes may be conservative but are tunable.

(c) Horizontal scaling: Multiple JVMs adds horizontal scaling but doesn't address intra-JVM efficiency. Best to tune both.

(d) Incorrect: Tuning is explicitly supported.

a.text: Spring Zeebe is multi-threaded; default pool sizes may be conservative but are tunable. — wrong; multi-threaded supported.

b.text: Tuning is explicitly supported. — ; configurable.

c.text: Multiple JVMs adds horizontal scaling but doesn't address intra-JVM efficiency. Best to tune both. — horizontal scaling; doesn't address intra-JVM tuning.

d.text: Intra-JVM parallelism in Spring Zeebe is controlled by two related knobs: (1) executor thread pool — how many threads in the JVM process activated jobs in parallel; (2) maxJobsActive — how many jobs the worker subscription activates at a time. For full utilisation, threads ≥ maxJobsActive (otherwise threads sit idle while jobs queue). Tune based on workload: CPU-bound work → match thread count to CPU cores; I/O-bound work → can over-subscribe (more threads than cores). — Thread pool + maxJobsActive tunes intra-JVM concurrency.

scenario: A TypeScript Job Worker using `@camunda8/sdk` handles a `validate-vat` task. The validator throws custom error classes like `InvalidVatError`, `RegionMismatchError`, `ExternalApiTimeoutError`. The team wants each custom error class to be mapped to a specific BPMN error code so the BPMN can route appropriately via Error Boundary Events.
question: Which pattern in the handler maps JS errors to BPMN errors with error codes?

options.text: Configure global error mapper in SDK options — some SDK versions expose this; check current docs. Cleaner for many error classes vs many handlers.
SDK auto-converts JS Error → BPMN error with errorCode = error class name — partial truth; some SDK versions may have heuristics, but explicit mapping is safer.
Don't catch; let the error propagate and Zeebe fails the job — loses the BPMN-error-with-code mapping; the BPMN can't route by code.

Wrap the handler body in try/catch; on caught error, inspect the error's class / type and call `job.error({errorCode, errorMessage})` with the appropriate code based on the error class. E.g., `InvalidVatError` → `errorCode: "INVALID_VAT"`. The BPMN's Error Boundary catches by errorCode.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Explicit try/catch + `job.error({...})` mapping is the canonical, reliable pattern. Inside the handler, wrap the logic; in catch, inspect `error instanceof InvalidVatError`, build the right errorCode/message, call `job.error()`. This produces a BPMN error event in Zeebe that propagates to a matching Error Boundary Event with that errorCode. Result: BPMN-level routing by error type.

(b) Avoid relying on heuristics: Even if some SDK versions auto-map, explicit mapping is more maintainable — the mapping is visible in code, doesn't depend on undocumented behaviour.

(c) Cleaner for scale: A global error mapper (configured at SDK init) centralises the JS-error-to-BPMN-error mapping for all workers. Useful when you have many error classes and many handlers; reduces duplication. Verify your SDK version supports this.

(d) Loses code mapping: Unhandled errors become generic Zeebe job failures (Incident with stack trace), not BPMN errors. BPMN can't route by code; ops must intervene manually.

a.text: A global error mapper (configured at SDK init) centralises the JS-error-to-BPMN-error mapping for all workers. Useful when you have many error classes and many handlers; reduces duplication. Verify your SDK version supports this. — valid alternative scale; verify SDK version.

b.text: Even if some SDK versions auto-map, explicit mapping is more maintainable — the mapping is visible in code, doesn't depend on undocumented behaviour. — partial; auto-mapping heuristic-dependent.

c.text: Unhandled errors become generic Zeebe job failures (Incident with stack trace), not BPMN errors. BPMN can't route by code; ops must intervene manually. — loses code mapping; manual incident handling needed.

d.text: Explicit try/catch + `job.error({...})` mapping is the canonical, reliable pattern. Inside the handler, wrap the logic; in catch, inspect `error instanceof InvalidVatError`, build the right errorCode/message, call `job.error()`. This produces a BPMN error event in Zeebe that propagates to a matching Error Boundary Event with that errorCode. Result: BPMN-level routing by error type. — Explicit try/catch + job.error mapping; clear and reliable.

scenario: Ops needs to know how many running instances of `daily-batch` exist at this moment for capacity planning. They want a programmatic check — not Operate UI clicks.
question: Which Camunda 8 API + endpoint fits?

options.text: Orchestration Cluster REST API — POST `/v2/process-instances/search` with filter `processDefinitionId = "daily-batch"` and `state = "ACTIVE"`. The response includes a list of matching instances; the response metadata (pagination) gives the total count.
Operate UI only — humans can browse, but ops wants programmatic.
Optimize API — Optimize is for historical analytics; not realtime counting of current active instances.
Zeebe gRPC API — Zeebe's gRPC is command-oriented (start instances, complete jobs, etc.), not query-oriented; query operations are in the Orchestration REST API.

explanation:
(a) Correct: The Orchestration Cluster REST API's `/v2/process-instances/search` endpoint is the canonical programmatic query. Filter by processDefinitionId and state; the response carries the matching list. For just the count, request `pageSize=0` or check the response metadata for the total. This API is the modern unified query interface (replaces legacy Operate/Tasklist API queries).

(b) UI only: Operate is for humans. For programmatic queries (CI checks, capacity planning, alerts), use the API.

(c) Wrong scope: Zeebe gRPC is command-side (write operations). Query-side is the Orchestration REST API.

(d) Wrong purpose: Optimize answers "how did instances behave historically?" — not "how many are running right now?"

a.text: The Orchestration Cluster REST API's `/v2/process-instances/search` endpoint is the canonical programmatic query. Filter by processDefinitionId and state; the response carries the matching list. For just the count, request `pageSize=0` or check the response metadata for the total. This API is the modern unified query interface (replaces legacy Operate/Tasklist API queries). — /v2/process-instances/search filtered by state.

b.text: Operate is for humans. For programmatic queries (CI checks, capacity planning, alerts), use the API. — UI works humans, programmatic.

c.text: Optimize answers "how did instances behave historically?" — not "how many are running right now?" — wrong purpose (analytics, realtime count).

d.text: Zeebe gRPC is command-side (write operations). Query-side is the Orchestration REST API. — wrong scope (command, query).

scenario: A custom Java Connector has an Element Template property bound via `zeebe:input` named `apiUrl`. The Connector function needs to read this at execution time.
question: Which Connector SDK API exposes the bound input to Java code?

options.text: Read from `System.getenv("apiUrl")` — incorrect; inputs aren't environment variables.
Parse BPMN XML at runtime — incorrect; SDK abstracts the BPMN.

Define a Java class with annotated fields representing the inputs; in `execute()`, call `context.bindVariables(InputClass.class)` (or similar SDK method) — the SDK reads the bound inputs into the class instance. Or use field-level annotations like `@TemplateProperty` for declarative binding.
Receive as a process variable in the Output Mapping — wrong direction; Output Mapping is for what comes OUT of the task, not what goes IN.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Connector SDK exposes a type-safe binding API. The canonical pattern: declare a Java class (e.g., `MyConnectorInput`) with fields matching template properties; in `execute(context)`, call `context.bindVariables(MyConnectorInput.class)` to get a populated instance. Annotations like `@TemplateProperty` declare per-field metadata (validation, default values, group). This approach is type-safe, IDE-supported, and refactor-friendly.

(b) Wrong source: Environment variables aren't Connector inputs. Connectors operate per-task-instance; env vars are JVM-process-wide.

(c) Wrong layer: SDK abstracts the BPMN; you don't parse it manually.

(d) Wrong direction: Output Mapping comes after task completion; inputs come from `zeebe:input` mappings + context.

a.text: Environment variables aren't Connector inputs. Connectors operate per-task-instance; env vars are JVM-process-wide. — wrong source (env vars).

b.text: SDK abstracts the BPMN; you don't parse it manually. — wrong layer.

c.text: Connector SDK exposes a type-safe binding API. The canonical pattern: declare a Java class (e.g., `MyConnectorInput`) with fields matching template properties; in `execute(context)`, call `context.bindVariables(MyConnectorInput.class)` to get a populated instance. Annotations like `@TemplateProperty` declare per-field metadata (validation, default values, group). This approach is type-safe, IDE-supported, and refactor-friendly. — context.bindVariables(InputClass) + @TemplateProperty annotations.

d.text: Output Mapping comes after task completion; inputs come from `zeebe:input` mappings + context. — wrong direction.

scenario: A team's worker logs show recurring `gRPC RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` errors when polling for jobs. The cluster is healthy — other workers in different namespaces don't see this error.
question: What does `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` typically indicate?

options.text: Process not deployed — different error path; would surface as `NOT_FOUND` or specific Zeebe error responses.
Auth failure — would be `UNAUTHENTICATED` or `PERMISSION_DENIED`.
Network outage — would typically be `UNAVAILABLE` or `DEADLINE_EXCEEDED`, not RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED.

Throttling / capacity limit — the gateway is rejecting requests because the worker is sending faster than the cluster's quota allows (per-tenant rate limit, gateway thread pool exhaustion, broker backpressure, etc.). Mitigations: reduce request rate, tune `maxJobsActive`, increase `pollInterval`, scale gateway, check cluster quotas. Documentation: [Zeebe API](https://docs.camunda.io/docs/apis-tools/zeebe-api/) + [gRPC status codes](https://grpc.github.io/grpc/core/md_doc_statuscodes.html)

explanation:
(a) Correct: gRPC's standard `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` (code 8) signals that some resource (rate quota, memory, threads, etc.) is at capacity. In Zeebe context, common causes: per-tenant rate limit hit, gateway thread pool saturated, broker applying backpressure. Diagnostic approach: check the cluster metrics, look at backpressure indicators, reduce the worker's request rate or increase the cluster's capacity.

(b) Different code: Network issues typically surface as UNAVAILABLE.

(c) Different code: Auth issues surface as UNAUTHENTICATED.

(d) Different code: Missing process would surface as NOT_FOUND when starting an instance of it.

a.text: Missing process would surface as NOT_FOUND when starting an instance of it. — would be NOT_FOUND.

b.text: Auth issues surface as UNAUTHENTICATED. — would be UNAUTHENTICATED.

c.text: Network issues typically surface as UNAVAILABLE. — would be UNAVAILABLE.

d.text: gRPC's standard `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` (code 8) signals that some resource (rate quota, memory, threads, etc.) is at capacity. In Zeebe context, common causes: per-tenant rate limit hit, gateway thread pool saturated, broker applying backpressure. Diagnostic approach: check the cluster metrics, look at backpressure indicators, reduce the worker's request rate or increase the cluster's capacity. — RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED = throttling / capacity limit.

scenario: A data team has Python scripts for analytics. They want to integrate with Camunda 8 — start process instances, query state, publish messages. They're looking for a Python SDK.
question: What's the canonical integration approach for Python?

options.text: Use the Orchestration Cluster REST API directly from Python — Python has excellent HTTP libraries (`requests`, `httpx`); the REST API is language-agnostic. Camunda 8 officially provides SDKs for Java, Node.js, Go (and others as community projects); for Python the REST API is the canonical path.
Python isn't supported with Camunda 8 — incorrect; REST API enables any HTTP-capable client.
Run the Java SDK via Jython — Jython is largely unmaintained; not a recommended path.
Camunda provides an official Python SDK with feature parity — historically not the case; verify per current version.

explanation:
(a) Correct: Camunda 8's REST API is the language-agnostic integration interface. Python integrates via standard HTTP libraries: handle OAuth2 token exchange (`requests-oauthlib` or manual), call `/v2/process-instances`, `/v2/messages/publication`, etc. For Job Workers in Python, the REST-based job activation is more complex than the SDK-managed flow but workable. Community Python SDKs exist (verify maintenance level before adopting).

(b) Verify per version: Camunda may add or recognise Python SDKs over time; always check current docs.

(c) Unmaintained route: Jython hasn't kept pace with modern Python; avoid.

(d) Incorrect: Python is supported via REST.

a.text: Camunda 8's REST API is the language-agnostic integration interface. Python integrates via standard HTTP libraries: handle OAuth2 token exchange (`requests-oauthlib` or manual), call `/v2/process-instances`, `/v2/messages/publication`, etc. For Job Workers in Python, the REST-based job activation is more complex than the SDK-managed flow but workable. Community Python SDKs exist (verify maintenance level before adopting). — REST API from Python standard HTTP libraries.

b.text: Python is supported via REST. — ; REST API enables any client.

c.text: Jython hasn't kept pace with modern Python; avoid. — Jython unmaintained.
